I have created three divs inside. counter 1, 2, and three. These will each. hold animated numbers that run during. the loading sequence. We'll create the. digits dynamically using JavaScript. later. Then we have got the images. container. This holds all the concept. images will be moving across the screen.

I have added 15 image wrappers here. You. can replace these with whatever visuals. you want to feature. Below that, I'm. adding a basic nav tag. On the left is. the logo name. In the center, we have. got some placeholder links. And on the. right, there is another placeholder. link. I've also added a div with the. class name divider underneath the nav,. which will animate in later for that. sleek horizontal line reveal. After that. comes the sidebar. It's positioned to. the left and contains a small logo and a. vertical divider line. We'll style it. later using CSS. Then we have the main heading area with. a class of header where I placed the. sides headline. Under that is the site info block. It. holds a short subheading, a horizontal. divider and two lines of meta content. just to give it that editorial feel. And finally, we have a footer-like. element called hero footer where I. placed some more dummy text. That's the. full HTML structure, clean, minimal, and. perfectly laid out for animation with. GZAP. Next, we'll move on to the CSS. part. You can see I've set up a few CSS. variables using the root selector. These. include background and foreground. colors, a loader background for the. intro animation, and a light stroke. color we'll use for the dividers. Then,. we'll reset the base styles, removing. all the margin and padding, and setting. box sizing to border box for consistent. layouts. For the body, I'm using a. modern sensory font called New Montreal. and applying the foreground color as the. default text color. Images are styled to. cover their containers using object fit. cover and stretched to full width and. height. Next, I'll style the text. For. H1, we'll go big and bold with a large. font size, reduced letter spacing, and a. tight line height to keep it looking. clean and editorial. H2 is slightly.

smaller, but follows the same visual. language, moderate weight, clean. spacing, and compact line height. Then,. for the links and paragraphs, I'm giving. them the same foreground color, no. underlines, and slightly bolder weight. Now we move on to the hero section which. spans the full view port and serves as. the canvas for the landing animation. It's set to position relative so that. all child elements can be absolutely. positioned within it and we clip any. overflow to keep animations contained. Inside the hero we have the hero. background which is a full screen. overlay used during the intro animation. It starts scale down on the y-axis and. expands upwards as the animation plays. Then we have the counter which is fixed. to the bottom right corner. This is our. preloader number ticker. It's styled with large text and we use. clip path to contain the scrolling. digits within the defined height. Each. digit column counter 1 2 and three is. nudged slightly to adjust the spacing. around fonts and we have some horizontal. offsets on specific digits to fine-tune. the appearance. Next is images container. which holds all the image cards we. animate in the intro. It's absolutely. positioned to fill the hero and each. image inside it is styled with a fixed. width, a cinematic aspect ratio, rounded. corners and hidden overflow to keep the. images neatly framed. There is also a special class called. animate out which shifts the images to. the bottom right corner after the flip. transition begins. We also define a generic divider class. with a subtle stroke background. This. gets reused in nav bars and sidebars as. a visual separator. Now we move on to. the navigation section. The nav bar is. horizontally padded and set up as a flex. container to space the logo and links. evenly. The links group has spacing between.

elements. and the divider underneath start scale. down so we can animate in later using. GSAP. Then we set up the sidebar which hugs. the left edge of the screen. and vertically centers a tiny logo at. the top. It includes a vertical divider that. grows from the top when the animation. begins. Next, we place the main header in the. header. It's positioned about a third from the. top and pushed in from the left, giving. us a clean hero layout with strong. hierarchy. On the right side, site info holds the. subheading and meta information. This is. vertically centered and stacked in a. neat column with spacing between items. Like before, the divider here is. initially scaled to zero. So we can. animate it in. At the very bottom, we place hero. footer. It anchors to the bottom left. and adds a nice finishing touch to the. hero. Now, here is an important utility. we'll need later. These are some classes. used in combination with GSAP's split. text plug-in. We hide overflow on each. line and push the text down using a. translate Y. This lets us animate each. line upwards one by one for that elegant. split text reveal. And finally, we have. a responsive media query that adjusts. spacing and font sizes for smaller. screens. It shrinks the heading sizes,. hides navigation links, enlarge the. image grid slightly, and repositions the. header and info blocks to keep. everything readable and centered on. tablets and phones. That wraps up the. CSS. Next, we'll move into JavaScript. and see how we bring it all to life with. GSAP animations and flip transition. We'll start by importing GAP along with. two plugins, flip and split text. Once that's done, we register both.

plugins with GSAP so we can start using. them in our animations. Now the first. function we are writing is setup text. splitting. Inside this, we are selecting. all the main text elements, heading,. paragraphs, and links because we'll be. animating them line by line. For each of. these elements, we use split text to. break them into individual lines and. assign the class line to each line. That. allows us to target them in a consistent. way later using CSS and GSAP. Then for each line, we grab its text. content and wrap it inside a span tag. This gives us a wrapper we can animate. with translate Y, which is what creates. that smooth vertical revel effect you. saw earlier. This setup is crucial. because it lays the foundation for all. our timeline based text animations that. we'll trigger later in the intro. Next,. we'll move into the counter setup and. then hook into the main timeline. We'll. define a function called create counter. digits. And inside that we start by. targeting the first counter column. counter one. Here we manually create two. divs, one for zero and one for one and. assign them the number class. We also. apply an additional offset class to the. second digit so it aligns nicely when. animated. Then we append them both to. the first counter. For the second. counter column, we loop from 0 to 10 and. dynamically create number divs. When the. loop reaches 10, we reset the content to. zero. So the count wraps visually. We. also place light offset class to the. digit one so the spacing feels just. right. Then we move on to the third. column. This one is meant to scroll. through more digits. So we create 30. numbers in total using the module. operator to loop through 0 to 9. repeatedly. And finally we add one last. zero at the end to help create a. seamless stop in the animation. This. whole setup gives us full control over. each digit and lets us animate them.

smoothly with gap in the next step. Now. let's animate the counter. We define a. reusable animate counter function that. takes in a counter element, a duration,. and an optional delay. First, we. calculate the height of a single digit. using client height. Then, we calculate the total distance. the counter needs to travel by. multiplying the number of digits by that. height minus one to account for the. start position. Then we animate the counter upwards. using Gab's true method. moving it vertically with a smooth power. tweezing. Now let's jump into animating the image. tag. We create a function called animate. images. This grabs all the image elements. and first removes the animate out class. from each to reset their position. Then we use the set flip to capture the. initial state of all the images. Next we add the animate out class back. which repositions the images and flip. will animate between the original and. new states. We create a main timeline. and use flips from method to interpolate. the movement of all images smoothly. This includes a stagger for a nice. cascading effect. Then we loop through each image. individually to add a custom scale. animation. Each image scales up quickly using power. three in. and then eases back down to its original. size with power three out easing. The scale up and down combo is what adds. that polished bounce to the transition. Finally, each of these scale timelines. is inserted into the main timeline with. a slight delay between each, making the. whole sequence feel responsive and. alive. This function returns the entire. animation timeline so it can be. triggered later when the loader.

completes. Now, let's bring everything. together inside the DOM content loaded. event listener. As soon as the page. finishes loading, we first call setup. text splitting function to wrap all text. elements with split text and then. initialize the counter digits using the. create counter digits function. Next, we. animate all three counters, each with. different durations and a slight delay. for the first digit so it appears in a. staggered countdown like motion. After. that, we set up a main gap timeline for. the overall reveal sequence. We begin by. collapsing the image elements to scale. zero just to prep them for the. animation. Then, we animate the hero. background to scale vertically from. bottom to top, revealing the background. in a smooth motion. Once the background. expands, we animate the image stack,. scaling them from zero to full size with. a nice stagger for that rising stack. effect. As soon as the image stack is in. place, we fade out the counter. This is. where we trigger the animate images. function to add the bouncing flip. animation we built earlier. Next up, we. reveal the vertical divider inside the. sidebar by scaling its height from 0 to. 100%. We follow that with the horizontal. dividers inside the nav and site info. sections. Scaling them along the x-axis. and a stagger to create pacing. Then the logo inside the sidebar scales. up to its full size. After that, we animate all the nav and. link texts. Since these were split using. split text, they animate upwards into. view line by line with a crisp stagger. and easing. And finally, I bring in the. headline, site info, and footer call. out. All sliding up in perfect sync. using the same animation strategy. The. sequence gives the whole landing page a. highly polished editorial style reveal. using just gzap timelines and a few. simple layout techniques. Hope you found. the video helpful. See you in the next.

one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uOMunkYvrAw

Create a modern animated landing page using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript—powered by GSAP Flip, SplitText, and timeline transitions for a seamless, dynamic reveal.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Konpo

Website: https://www.konpo.studio/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!

item.title: The Most Aesthetic Content Slider That Warps Frames Like Liquid Glass (GSAP & Three JS)
item.itunes:title: The Most Aesthetic Content Slider That Warps Frames Like Liquid Glass (GSAP & Three JS)
item.itunes:author: Codegrid
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 深入了解如何使用 Three.js、自訂 Shaders 和 GSAP 共同打造引人注目的網頁動畫效果，特別是帶有鏡頭扭曲的內容滑塊。
  • 學習如何將複雜的專案分解為獨立的部分：HTML 結構、CSS 樣式、資料管理、Shader 編寫（即使沒有背景，也可藉助工具）和 JavaScript 邏輯。
  • 探索如何準備和控制文字元素的動畫（例如使用 GSAP SplitText 插件），以實現精確的進場和離場效果。
  • 瞭解如何設定 WebGL 環境並將自訂 Shader 應用於紋理過渡動畫。

摘要

作者在尋找值得學習的精彩網頁動畫時，被一個網站上帶有鏡頭扭曲效果的內容滑塊深深吸引。儘管過去他傾向於避免使用 Shaders，但對這種獨特扭曲效果的好奇心促使他決定嘗試自己重建。他投入了大約八小時的時間進行嘗試，並成功建立了一個基本版本的滑塊，該版本具有相似的失真過渡效果，雖然不及原作精緻，但他認為這個過程值得分享。

因此，在本次影片中，作者將詳細分解如何使用 Three.js、自訂 Shaders 和 GSAP 來建立這種動畫內容滑塊的整個流程。

設定基礎結構與樣式

作者首先設定了基礎的 HTML 結構，力求簡潔。他創建了一個主要的容器 `slider`，內部包含一個用於 WebGL 扭曲過渡的 canvas 元素，以及一個疊加在 canvas 上方、用於顯示文字內容的 `slider-content` 容器。

`slider-content` 中包含了標題 (`h1`)、描述 (`p`) 和額外的元資訊（類型、領域、日期）。作者強調這是一個非常基礎的結構，所有動態內容和效果都將由 JavaScript 和 Shaders 控制。

接著，作者設定了 CSS 樣式。他從基本的樣式重設和字體導入開始，然後設定了 `slider` 容器為全視口大小、相對定位並隱藏溢出。Canvas 被設定為全寬高區塊元素，以確保填滿空間。

`slider-content` 則使用絕對定位疊加在 canvas 上方，並透過 z-index 確保其顯示在最上層。作者調整了標題的居中和描述的位置樣式，並使用了 Flexbox 來排列描述中的詳細資訊。

一個關鍵的部分是為 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件準備的實用工具樣式。作者為標題（字元）和描述（行）的文字元素設定了特定的 display 和 overflow 屬性，以便能精確地動畫每個字元和行，確保動畫乾淨流暢。

內容資料與 Shaders

為了保持程式碼整潔，作者將幻燈片內容儲存在一個獨立的 `slides.js` 檔案中，這是一個包含標題、描述、圖片路徑等資訊的物件陣列，這種方式方便管理和未來的動態載入。

Shaders 的部分也放在另一個 `shaders.js` 檔案中。作者說明 Vertex Shader 比較簡單，主要負責傳遞 UV 座標，而所有的視覺魔法都發生在 Fragment Shader 中。

作者坦承自己沒有編寫 Shader 的背景，並提到這個 Fragment Shader 是在 Claude AI 的幫助下完成的，用於實現影片開頭提到的圓形鏡頭扭曲過渡效果。

這個 Shader 處理當前和下一張幻燈片的圖片紋理，使用一個逐漸擴大的圓形遮罩進行過渡，並對新進來的圖片應用柔和的鏡頭扭曲。它還包含一個 `coverUV` 函式來幫助圖片正確地覆蓋視口，並暴露一個名為 `progress` 的 uniform 變數，這個變數將透過 GSAP 動畫來控制整個過渡效果的進度。

JavaScript 主邏輯：設定與文字處理

在主要的 JavaScript 檔案中，作者首先匯入了前面建立的幻燈片資料、Shaders 程式碼，以及核心庫 Three.js 和 GSAP (包括 SplitText 插件)。

他註冊了 SplitText 插件並設定了一些全域變數來管理應用程式的狀態，例如當前幻燈片索引、一個用於防止重複過渡的旗標，以及儲存已載入紋理和 Shader 材質的變數。

接著，作者建立了一些文字處理工具函式。`createCharacterElements` 函式手動將標題中的每個字元包裹在多個 span 標籤中，以便能夠精確地控制每一個字元的動畫。

`createLineElements` 函式則利用 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件來將段落分割成行，這使得可以輕鬆地動畫整行的文字。

最後，一個名為 `processTextElements` 的輔助函式整合了前兩者，這個函式會找到 H1 標題並應用字元分割，然後找到描述中的所有段落並應用行分割，這樣在載入新幻燈片時只需呼叫這個函式就能準備好所有文字元素進行動畫。

幻燈片元素建立

作者開始說明如何處理實際的幻燈片內容的創建和動態插入。他介紹了一個函式 `createSlideElement`，這個函式接收特定幻燈片的資料（標題、描述等），並返回一個結構完整的 DOM 元素，代表該幻燈片的內容區塊。

這個函式會動態創建一個帶有 `slider-content` 類的 div，並在其中注入根據資料生成的 HTML 結構，包括標題、描述和元資訊。初始創建時，這個元素的透明度會被設為零，以便稍後通過動畫使其淡入。

================================================

For a while now, this site has been. sitting on my list of must do breakdowns. as it's packed with some incredible. animations. Among everything it. showcases, what really stood out to me. was this sleek content slider. As you. scroll, the images shift with a striking. lens distortion effect. While the. content glides out and the next one. flows in, super smooth and visually. captivating. What made this especially. challenging was this distortion. animation. I would never built anything. quite like it. A few years back, I was. actively avoiding shaders, but the. curiosity about how these warped effects. work never really left me. Lately. though, we have started exploring that. space more here on the channel. So, I. figured this was the perfect time to. finally try building it myself. So, I. gave it a shot and after hours of. tinkering, I managed to put together a. basic version of the slider. It triggers. on click and carries a similar. distortion transition. Not exactly the. same, but pretty close. It's not as. polished as the original, but. considering it only took about 8 hours. to rebuild, I figured it was worth. sharing the full breakdown. So, in. today's video, we'll walk through how to. build this kind of animated content. slider using 3JS, custom shaders, and. GAP. If you enjoy seeing award-winning. animations broken down and rebuilt from. scratch, make sure to like the video and. subscribe to the channel. And if you. want to access the source code for this. project, plus hundreds of other similar. micro projects and a brand new full. website template every month, you can. check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's get into it. Let's start by setting up the HTML. structure. We'll keep it minimal, just a. full screen slider wrapper that holds a. canvas for the distortion transition and. a content container layered on top of. it. So, I'll create a slider div first. This will be our main wrapper. Inside. that we'll add a canvas tag. We'll use. this later for the WebGL shader. animation. Then right after that, I'll. drop in a slider content div. This is. where we'll display the text content for. each slide like the title and. description. Inside the content, we'll have a slide. title with an H1 and a slide description. that includes a paragraph and some meta. info, type, field, and date. That's all. we need here, just a clean base.

structure. Everything else will be. driven through JavaScript and shaders. Let's move on to the styling. First, I've imported the inter font from. Google fonts. Then we'll start by. resetting the basic styles, removing. default margin and padding and setting. box sizing to border box for. consistency. For the body, I'll apply. the inter font. Now, for the headings,. we'll style the H1 to be all uppercase. Give it a bold weight and scale it up. with a flute font size that adapts to. the view port. We'll also tighten the. line height for a sharper look. paragraphs get a slightly smaller size. to keep them clean and readable. Next, I'll style the slider container. It's going to be full view port width. and height position relative with. overflow hidden and white text by. default. Inside that, the canvas will be full. width and height and set to block. So,. it fills the space without any scroll. bars. Now the content that sits on top. of the canvas will target the slider. content and absolutely position it to. cover the full area. We'll also prevent. user selection and bump its C index up. so it stays above the canvas. The slide title will be centered both. vertically and horizontally using a. transform translate and we'll align the. text in the center. Then for the description block, I'll. move it slightly below the title. Offset it a bit to one side and give it. a fixed width. I'll use flex direction column and space. out the children using a nice gap. For the extra meta info, I'll transform. the text to uppercase to match the. editorial wipe. Now comes the important. part for animations. Since we'll be. using Gap's split text plug-in to break. down the title into characters and the. paragraph into lines, I'll add some. utility styles for that. So, for the. title, I'll make sure each word is. displayed as a flex row and each. character block is set to block display. Then, for both characters and lines,. I'll hide overflow so we can animate. them cleanly in and out. And finally,. the spans inside those characters and. lines, we'll set them to inline block. enable will change on transform for. performance and make sure they're. positioned relative so we can animate. them smoothly. Lastly, I'll throw in a. quick media query to adjust the layout. on smaller screens. We'll tweak the. title and description positions and.

widen the content for better legibility. on mobile. And that's it for the. styling. Now, I have created a separate file. called slides.js just to keep things. clean. In here, I set up an array of. objects. Each one represents a slide. It. includes a title, a description, type,. field, date, and an image path. Keeping. the content separate like this makes it. super easy to manage later, especially. if you want to pull in data dynamically. or hook it up with the CMS down the. line. Now, let's talk about the shaders. There is another file called shaders.js,. which holds both vortex and fragment. shaders. These are just exported strings. that we'll inject into 3JS when setting. up our shader material. The vortex. shader is super simple. It just passes. the UV coordinates through so we can use. them in the fragment shader. Nothing. fancy here. But the fragment shader,. that's where all the magic happens. And. I mentioned this many times before. I. don't have any background in writing. shaders. So for this part, I got some. help from Claude. I basically explained. the visual effect I wanted something. like a circular lens distortion that. grows during the transition. And Claude. helped me write a GLSL shader based on. that. The shader takes two textures. our. current slide image and the next one. Then it uses circular mask to animate. the transition. While the circle grows,. it applies this soft lens distortion on. the incoming image to give that liquid. warped feel. There is also a function in. there that helps us scale and crop the. images properly so they always cover the. viewport without getting stretched. That's handled with a custom cover UV. function. And finally, I have exposed a. progress uniform that will animate with. gap to control the whole effect frame by. frame. We'll pass all these uniforms. through 3JS and update them on every. transition. All right, now that we have. got content and shaders out of the way,. let's wire things up in the main script. file. First, I'll import the data and shaders. So, we'll bring in the slides array from. the slides JS file. And then we'll. import both the vortex shader and. fragment shader from our shaders file. After that, we bring in 3JS which we'll. use to handle all the WebGL rendering. and apply custom shaders. Then we import. gap and also it split text plug-in. We'll be using this plug-in to break. down the title into individual. characters and the paragraph into.

separate lines so we can animate them. one by one. Now, right after the. imports, I'll register the split text. plug-in and turn off null target. warnings. That's just to clean up the. console a bit since GP might. occasionally target things that don't. exist yet, especially during. transitions. Then I'll set up a few. global variables to manage state. We. have got current slide index which keeps. track of which slide is currently active. as transitioning is a flag we'll use to. avoid triggering new transitions while. an animation is already playing. We'll. also store all the loaded textures in. slide textures and declared shader. material and renderer so we can. initialize them later and still access. them throughout the script. All right,. now let's handle the text splitting. We'll start with a utility function. called create character elements. This. takes a DOM element like our title and. manually wraps each character in span. tags. We are doing this because we want. the full control over every single. letters animation. So inside this. function we first check if the element. already has character elements. If it. does we return early so we don't double. wrap anything. Then we split the text. into words and for each word we create a. wpper div with the class word. Inside. that we loop through every character and. wrap it in its own character div. and inside that a span. That span is. what we'll animate later using GAP. We. also add an empty space character. between words so spacing looks natural. Now for line splitting, we have got. another function called create line. elements. This one uses Gap's split text. plug-in directly to break a paragraph. into lines and then wraps each line in a. span. Again, this lets us animate full lines. in and out with clean vertical motion. And finally, we wrap those two into an. helper function called process text. elements. This looks for the H1 title and runs it. through the character splitter. Then. finds all the paragraph tags in the. description and runs those through the. line splitter. This way whenever we load. a new slide, we can just call this one. function to prepare all the text for. animation. All right, now that we have. got text splitting utilities out of the. way, let's move on and handle how the. actual slide content gets created and. animated. We'll start with a function. called create slide element. This takes.

in the slide data like title,. description and so on and returns a. fully structured DOM element. So first. I'm creating a new div and giving it the. class slider content. I am setting its. opacity to zero for now. We'll fade it. in later as part of the animation. Then. inside this element I'm injecting the. full HTML layout for the slide that. includes the title inside an H1, the. main description inside a paragraph tag. and then extra details type field and. date inside their own container. This. structure is the same as what we had in. our HTML file earlier. It just gets. dynamically created. Now, every time we. switch slides, once that's returned, we. can append it to the tom whenever we. need a new slide. Now, let's look at how. we handle the actual animation between. slides. So, here is our animate slide. transition function. It takes in the. index of the next slide we want to show. First, I grab the current content. element and also the main slider. wrapper. Then, I create a gap timeline. We'll use this to sequence everything in. order. The first step is to animate out. the current content. So we target all. the character spans from the title and. slide them upward by animating the Y-. value. We give them a slight stagger so. the letters flow out one after another. Right after that, we do the same thing. for the paragraph lines. Again, sliding. them up with a bit of delay and a smooth. easing. Now once those are out of the. way, we run a call function inside the. timeline. This lets us inject custom. logic into the animation sequence. Inside that block, we create the next. content element using the create slide. element function we wrote earlier. Then. we kill the old timeline to stop any. leftover animations. Remove the old. slide from the DOM and append the new. one in its place. Next, we prepare the. new content for animation. We set all. these spans inside it to be pushed down. so they are hidden initially. We give it. a short delay just to ensure everything. is in place and then run process text. elements function to break the new title. and description into animatable chunks. just like before. Then we grab all the. new character and spans and animate them. into view, sliding everything up from. below. Once the animation is done, we. reset the is transitioning flag and. update the current slide index so we. know which slide is currently active. So. that's the full transition from fading. out the old content, loading the new. one, breaking it into pieces, and.

sliding everything back in. Next, we'll. look at how to set up the initial slide. and wire up the renderer. We'll start with the setup initial slide. function. This one just runs when the. page loads and it animates the very. first slide into view. So first I grab. the slider content element from the DOM. Then we call our process text elements. helper to split the title into. characters and the description into. lines just like we have been doing. during transitions. Next, we grab all. the individual spans for characters and. lines and then we animate them in using. gap. The characters comes up from below. with a bit of stagger for that fluid. motion. and the lines follow shortly after with. a small delay. This gives the first. light that smooth staggered entrance as. soon as the page loads. Now let's look at the main renderer. setup. We are creating a function called. initialize renderer. This is where all. the 3JS and shader logic kicks in. First, we create a new 3JS scene and a. basic orthographic camera. We don't need. perspective here since we are just doing. flat full screen rendering. Then, we. initialize the renderer targeting the. canvas element we placed in our HTML. earlier. I'm enabling anti-aliasing to smooth out. the edges a bit. We also set the render. size to match the current window size. Now, here comes the important part,. setting up the shader material. We are. creating a new shader material and. passing in all the uniforms we need for. our distortion effect. So we have got. uniform texture one and uniform texture. 2 for the current and next images,. uniform progress to animate the. distortion, uniform resolution to keep. track of the viewport size and the size. of both textures so we can scale them. properly inside the shader. Then we add a full screen plane mesh to. the scene using the shader material. This will render our distortion effect. directly to the canvas. Now let's load the textures. I'm using. texture loader to load all the slide. images from our array. For each one, I'm storing it in an array. and also saving the original image. dimensions so we can pass them to the. shader later. Once all the textures are loaded, we. assign the first two, slide one and. slide two as the default images for. uniform texture one and uniform texture. 2. This sets up the initial state of the.

canvas. Finally, we start the render loop. I'll. define a render function that just calls. itself with request animation frame and. renders the scene with the camera on. each frame. And that's it. Our WebGL. canvas is ready. The distortion shader. is running and we are good to trigger. transitions. Next, we'll wire up the. click event and the transition trigger. We'll start with the handle slide change. function. This gets triggered every time. we click on the page. The first thing we. do is check if a transition is already. in progress. If it is, we return early. and ignore the click just to avoid. overlapping animations. Then we set it. transitioning flag to true so nothing. else can interrupt while the transition. is running. Next, we calculate the next. slide index. So we simply increment the. current slide index and wrap around. using modulo. This gives us an endless. loop of slides. Now we update the shader. uniforms. We assign the current and next. image textures to texture one and. texture two. And we also update the size. values for both textures so the shader. can scale them correctly. Once that's. done, we call the animate slide. transition function we wrote earlier. This will handle animating the text. content between slides. And while that's. happening, we also animate the shader. itself. We use gap to animate the. progress uniform from 0 to one over a. couple seconds with a nice easing. This. drives the distortion effect inside the. fragment shader. So while the content is. sliding out and the new one is coming. in, the image beneath also gets. distorted and transitions to the next. one. Once the shader animation. completes, we reset progress uniform. back to zero and update texture one to. hold the new current image. So we are. ready for the next transition. Now we'll. add a resize handler just to make the. canvas responsive. In handle resize. function, we update the renderer size. and also refresh the resolution uniform. so the shader knows the new viewport. dimensions. Finally, we hook everything up on window. load. We call setup initial slide to. animate the first text in and then. initialize renderer to boot up the. canvas and load the textures. We also add a click listener to trigger. the slide change and a resize listener. to keep things fluid. And that's it. The whole setup is done. We have got a full screen content slider. animated with GSAP and powered by custom.

shaders in 3JS. Hope you found the video. helpful. See you in the next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7TcfWFh4ATA

Learn how to build a responsive animated content slider using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Three.js, custom shaders, and GSAP—with liquid lens distortion and smooth text transitions.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Oscar Pico

Website: https://www.oscarpico.es/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.description:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 深入了解如何使用 Three.js、自訂 Shaders 和 GSAP 共同打造引人注目的網頁動畫效果，特別是帶有鏡頭扭曲的內容滑塊。
  • 學習如何將複雜的專案分解為獨立的部分：HTML 結構、CSS 樣式、資料管理、Shader 編寫（即使沒有背景，也可藉助工具）和 JavaScript 邏輯。
  • 探索如何準備和控制文字元素的動畫（例如使用 GSAP SplitText 插件），以實現精確的進場和離場效果。
  • 瞭解如何設定 WebGL 環境並將自訂 Shader 應用於紋理過渡動畫。

摘要

作者在尋找值得學習的精彩網頁動畫時，被一個網站上帶有鏡頭扭曲效果的內容滑塊深深吸引。儘管過去他傾向於避免使用 Shaders，但對這種獨特扭曲效果的好奇心促使他決定嘗試自己重建。他投入了大約八小時的時間進行嘗試，並成功建立了一個基本版本的滑塊，該版本具有相似的失真過渡效果，雖然不及原作精緻，但他認為這個過程值得分享。

因此，在本次影片中，作者將詳細分解如何使用 Three.js、自訂 Shaders 和 GSAP 來建立這種動畫內容滑塊的整個流程。

設定基礎結構與樣式

作者首先設定了基礎的 HTML 結構，力求簡潔。他創建了一個主要的容器 `slider`，內部包含一個用於 WebGL 扭曲過渡的 canvas 元素，以及一個疊加在 canvas 上方、用於顯示文字內容的 `slider-content` 容器。

`slider-content` 中包含了標題 (`h1`)、描述 (`p`) 和額外的元資訊（類型、領域、日期）。作者強調這是一個非常基礎的結構，所有動態內容和效果都將由 JavaScript 和 Shaders 控制。

接著，作者設定了 CSS 樣式。他從基本的樣式重設和字體導入開始，然後設定了 `slider` 容器為全視口大小、相對定位並隱藏溢出。Canvas 被設定為全寬高區塊元素，以確保填滿空間。

`slider-content` 則使用絕對定位疊加在 canvas 上方，並透過 z-index 確保其顯示在最上層。作者調整了標題的居中和描述的位置樣式，並使用了 Flexbox 來排列描述中的詳細資訊。

一個關鍵的部分是為 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件準備的實用工具樣式。作者為標題（字元）和描述（行）的文字元素設定了特定的 display 和 overflow 屬性，以便能精確地動畫每個字元和行，確保動畫乾淨流暢。

內容資料與 Shaders

為了保持程式碼整潔，作者將幻燈片內容儲存在一個獨立的 `slides.js` 檔案中，這是一個包含標題、描述、圖片路徑等資訊的物件陣列，這種方式方便管理和未來的動態載入。

Shaders 的部分也放在另一個 `shaders.js` 檔案中。作者說明 Vertex Shader 比較簡單，主要負責傳遞 UV 座標，而所有的視覺魔法都發生在 Fragment Shader 中。

作者坦承自己沒有編寫 Shader 的背景，並提到這個 Fragment Shader 是在 Claude AI 的幫助下完成的，用於實現影片開頭提到的圓形鏡頭扭曲過渡效果。

這個 Shader 處理當前和下一張幻燈片的圖片紋理，使用一個逐漸擴大的圓形遮罩進行過渡，並對新進來的圖片應用柔和的鏡頭扭曲。它還包含一個 `coverUV` 函式來幫助圖片正確地覆蓋視口，並暴露一個名為 `progress` 的 uniform 變數，這個變數將透過 GSAP 動畫來控制整個過渡效果的進度。

JavaScript 主邏輯：設定與文字處理

在主要的 JavaScript 檔案中，作者首先匯入了前面建立的幻燈片資料、Shaders 程式碼，以及核心庫 Three.js 和 GSAP (包括 SplitText 插件)。

他註冊了 SplitText 插件並設定了一些全域變數來管理應用程式的狀態，例如當前幻燈片索引、一個用於防止重複過渡的旗標，以及儲存已載入紋理和 Shader 材質的變數。

接著，作者建立了一些文字處理工具函式。`createCharacterElements` 函式手動將標題中的每個字元包裹在多個 span 標籤中，以便能夠精確地控制每一個字元的動畫。

`createLineElements` 函式則利用 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件來將段落分割成行，這使得可以輕鬆地動畫整行的文字。

最後，一個名為 `processTextElements` 的輔助函式整合了前兩者，這個函式會找到 H1 標題並應用字元分割，然後找到描述中的所有段落並應用行分割，這樣在載入新幻燈片時只需呼叫這個函式就能準備好所有文字元素進行動畫。

幻燈片元素建立

作者開始說明如何處理實際的幻燈片內容的創建和動態插入。他介紹了一個函式 `createSlideElement`，這個函式接收特定幻燈片的資料（標題、描述等），並返回一個結構完整的 DOM 元素，代表該幻燈片的內容區塊。

這個函式會動態創建一個帶有 `slider-content` 類的 div，並在其中注入根據資料生成的 HTML 結構，包括標題、描述和元資訊。初始創建時，這個元素的透明度會被設為零，以便稍後通過動畫使其淡入。

================================================

For a while now, this site has been. sitting on my list of must do breakdowns. as it's packed with some incredible. animations. Among everything it. showcases, what really stood out to me. was this sleek content slider. As you. scroll, the images shift with a striking. lens distortion effect. While the. content glides out and the next one. flows in, super smooth and visually. captivating. What made this especially. challenging was this distortion. animation. I would never built anything. quite like it. A few years back, I was. actively avoiding shaders, but the. curiosity about how these warped effects. work never really left me. Lately. though, we have started exploring that. space more here on the channel. So, I. figured this was the perfect time to. finally try building it myself. So, I. gave it a shot and after hours of. tinkering, I managed to put together a. basic version of the slider. It triggers. on click and carries a similar. distortion transition. Not exactly the. same, but pretty close. It's not as. polished as the original, but. considering it only took about 8 hours. to rebuild, I figured it was worth. sharing the full breakdown. So, in. today's video, we'll walk through how to. build this kind of animated content. slider using 3JS, custom shaders, and. GAP. If you enjoy seeing award-winning. animations broken down and rebuilt from. scratch, make sure to like the video and. subscribe to the channel. And if you. want to access the source code for this. project, plus hundreds of other similar. micro projects and a brand new full. website template every month, you can. check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's get into it. Let's start by setting up the HTML. structure. We'll keep it minimal, just a. full screen slider wrapper that holds a. canvas for the distortion transition and. a content container layered on top of. it. So, I'll create a slider div first. This will be our main wrapper. Inside. that we'll add a canvas tag. We'll use. this later for the WebGL shader. animation. Then right after that, I'll. drop in a slider content div. This is. where we'll display the text content for. each slide like the title and. description. Inside the content, we'll have a slide. title with an H1 and a slide description. that includes a paragraph and some meta. info, type, field, and date. That's all. we need here, just a clean base.

structure. Everything else will be. driven through JavaScript and shaders. Let's move on to the styling. First, I've imported the inter font from. Google fonts. Then we'll start by. resetting the basic styles, removing. default margin and padding and setting. box sizing to border box for. consistency. For the body, I'll apply. the inter font. Now, for the headings,. we'll style the H1 to be all uppercase. Give it a bold weight and scale it up. with a flute font size that adapts to. the view port. We'll also tighten the. line height for a sharper look. paragraphs get a slightly smaller size. to keep them clean and readable. Next, I'll style the slider container. It's going to be full view port width. and height position relative with. overflow hidden and white text by. default. Inside that, the canvas will be full. width and height and set to block. So,. it fills the space without any scroll. bars. Now the content that sits on top. of the canvas will target the slider. content and absolutely position it to. cover the full area. We'll also prevent. user selection and bump its C index up. so it stays above the canvas. The slide title will be centered both. vertically and horizontally using a. transform translate and we'll align the. text in the center. Then for the description block, I'll. move it slightly below the title. Offset it a bit to one side and give it. a fixed width. I'll use flex direction column and space. out the children using a nice gap. For the extra meta info, I'll transform. the text to uppercase to match the. editorial wipe. Now comes the important. part for animations. Since we'll be. using Gap's split text plug-in to break. down the title into characters and the. paragraph into lines, I'll add some. utility styles for that. So, for the. title, I'll make sure each word is. displayed as a flex row and each. character block is set to block display. Then, for both characters and lines,. I'll hide overflow so we can animate. them cleanly in and out. And finally,. the spans inside those characters and. lines, we'll set them to inline block. enable will change on transform for. performance and make sure they're. positioned relative so we can animate. them smoothly. Lastly, I'll throw in a. quick media query to adjust the layout. on smaller screens. We'll tweak the. title and description positions and.

widen the content for better legibility. on mobile. And that's it for the. styling. Now, I have created a separate file. called slides.js just to keep things. clean. In here, I set up an array of. objects. Each one represents a slide. It. includes a title, a description, type,. field, date, and an image path. Keeping. the content separate like this makes it. super easy to manage later, especially. if you want to pull in data dynamically. or hook it up with the CMS down the. line. Now, let's talk about the shaders. There is another file called shaders.js,. which holds both vortex and fragment. shaders. These are just exported strings. that we'll inject into 3JS when setting. up our shader material. The vortex. shader is super simple. It just passes. the UV coordinates through so we can use. them in the fragment shader. Nothing. fancy here. But the fragment shader,. that's where all the magic happens. And. I mentioned this many times before. I. don't have any background in writing. shaders. So for this part, I got some. help from Claude. I basically explained. the visual effect I wanted something. like a circular lens distortion that. grows during the transition. And Claude. helped me write a GLSL shader based on. that. The shader takes two textures. our. current slide image and the next one. Then it uses circular mask to animate. the transition. While the circle grows,. it applies this soft lens distortion on. the incoming image to give that liquid. warped feel. There is also a function in. there that helps us scale and crop the. images properly so they always cover the. viewport without getting stretched. That's handled with a custom cover UV. function. And finally, I have exposed a. progress uniform that will animate with. gap to control the whole effect frame by. frame. We'll pass all these uniforms. through 3JS and update them on every. transition. All right, now that we have. got content and shaders out of the way,. let's wire things up in the main script. file. First, I'll import the data and shaders. So, we'll bring in the slides array from. the slides JS file. And then we'll. import both the vortex shader and. fragment shader from our shaders file. After that, we bring in 3JS which we'll. use to handle all the WebGL rendering. and apply custom shaders. Then we import. gap and also it split text plug-in. We'll be using this plug-in to break. down the title into individual. characters and the paragraph into.

separate lines so we can animate them. one by one. Now, right after the. imports, I'll register the split text. plug-in and turn off null target. warnings. That's just to clean up the. console a bit since GP might. occasionally target things that don't. exist yet, especially during. transitions. Then I'll set up a few. global variables to manage state. We. have got current slide index which keeps. track of which slide is currently active. as transitioning is a flag we'll use to. avoid triggering new transitions while. an animation is already playing. We'll. also store all the loaded textures in. slide textures and declared shader. material and renderer so we can. initialize them later and still access. them throughout the script. All right,. now let's handle the text splitting. We'll start with a utility function. called create character elements. This. takes a DOM element like our title and. manually wraps each character in span. tags. We are doing this because we want. the full control over every single. letters animation. So inside this. function we first check if the element. already has character elements. If it. does we return early so we don't double. wrap anything. Then we split the text. into words and for each word we create a. wpper div with the class word. Inside. that we loop through every character and. wrap it in its own character div. and inside that a span. That span is. what we'll animate later using GAP. We. also add an empty space character. between words so spacing looks natural. Now for line splitting, we have got. another function called create line. elements. This one uses Gap's split text. plug-in directly to break a paragraph. into lines and then wraps each line in a. span. Again, this lets us animate full lines. in and out with clean vertical motion. And finally, we wrap those two into an. helper function called process text. elements. This looks for the H1 title and runs it. through the character splitter. Then. finds all the paragraph tags in the. description and runs those through the. line splitter. This way whenever we load. a new slide, we can just call this one. function to prepare all the text for. animation. All right, now that we have. got text splitting utilities out of the. way, let's move on and handle how the. actual slide content gets created and. animated. We'll start with a function. called create slide element. This takes.

in the slide data like title,. description and so on and returns a. fully structured DOM element. So first. I'm creating a new div and giving it the. class slider content. I am setting its. opacity to zero for now. We'll fade it. in later as part of the animation. Then. inside this element I'm injecting the. full HTML layout for the slide that. includes the title inside an H1, the. main description inside a paragraph tag. and then extra details type field and. date inside their own container. This. structure is the same as what we had in. our HTML file earlier. It just gets. dynamically created. Now, every time we. switch slides, once that's returned, we. can append it to the tom whenever we. need a new slide. Now, let's look at how. we handle the actual animation between. slides. So, here is our animate slide. transition function. It takes in the. index of the next slide we want to show. First, I grab the current content. element and also the main slider. wrapper. Then, I create a gap timeline. We'll use this to sequence everything in. order. The first step is to animate out. the current content. So we target all. the character spans from the title and. slide them upward by animating the Y-. value. We give them a slight stagger so. the letters flow out one after another. Right after that, we do the same thing. for the paragraph lines. Again, sliding. them up with a bit of delay and a smooth. easing. Now once those are out of the. way, we run a call function inside the. timeline. This lets us inject custom. logic into the animation sequence. Inside that block, we create the next. content element using the create slide. element function we wrote earlier. Then. we kill the old timeline to stop any. leftover animations. Remove the old. slide from the DOM and append the new. one in its place. Next, we prepare the. new content for animation. We set all. these spans inside it to be pushed down. so they are hidden initially. We give it. a short delay just to ensure everything. is in place and then run process text. elements function to break the new title. and description into animatable chunks. just like before. Then we grab all the. new character and spans and animate them. into view, sliding everything up from. below. Once the animation is done, we. reset the is transitioning flag and. update the current slide index so we. know which slide is currently active. So. that's the full transition from fading. out the old content, loading the new. one, breaking it into pieces, and.

sliding everything back in. Next, we'll. look at how to set up the initial slide. and wire up the renderer. We'll start with the setup initial slide. function. This one just runs when the. page loads and it animates the very. first slide into view. So first I grab. the slider content element from the DOM. Then we call our process text elements. helper to split the title into. characters and the description into. lines just like we have been doing. during transitions. Next, we grab all. the individual spans for characters and. lines and then we animate them in using. gap. The characters comes up from below. with a bit of stagger for that fluid. motion. and the lines follow shortly after with. a small delay. This gives the first. light that smooth staggered entrance as. soon as the page loads. Now let's look at the main renderer. setup. We are creating a function called. initialize renderer. This is where all. the 3JS and shader logic kicks in. First, we create a new 3JS scene and a. basic orthographic camera. We don't need. perspective here since we are just doing. flat full screen rendering. Then, we. initialize the renderer targeting the. canvas element we placed in our HTML. earlier. I'm enabling anti-aliasing to smooth out. the edges a bit. We also set the render. size to match the current window size. Now, here comes the important part,. setting up the shader material. We are. creating a new shader material and. passing in all the uniforms we need for. our distortion effect. So we have got. uniform texture one and uniform texture. 2 for the current and next images,. uniform progress to animate the. distortion, uniform resolution to keep. track of the viewport size and the size. of both textures so we can scale them. properly inside the shader. Then we add a full screen plane mesh to. the scene using the shader material. This will render our distortion effect. directly to the canvas. Now let's load the textures. I'm using. texture loader to load all the slide. images from our array. For each one, I'm storing it in an array. and also saving the original image. dimensions so we can pass them to the. shader later. Once all the textures are loaded, we. assign the first two, slide one and. slide two as the default images for. uniform texture one and uniform texture. 2. This sets up the initial state of the.

canvas. Finally, we start the render loop. I'll. define a render function that just calls. itself with request animation frame and. renders the scene with the camera on. each frame. And that's it. Our WebGL. canvas is ready. The distortion shader. is running and we are good to trigger. transitions. Next, we'll wire up the. click event and the transition trigger. We'll start with the handle slide change. function. This gets triggered every time. we click on the page. The first thing we. do is check if a transition is already. in progress. If it is, we return early. and ignore the click just to avoid. overlapping animations. Then we set it. transitioning flag to true so nothing. else can interrupt while the transition. is running. Next, we calculate the next. slide index. So we simply increment the. current slide index and wrap around. using modulo. This gives us an endless. loop of slides. Now we update the shader. uniforms. We assign the current and next. image textures to texture one and. texture two. And we also update the size. values for both textures so the shader. can scale them correctly. Once that's. done, we call the animate slide. transition function we wrote earlier. This will handle animating the text. content between slides. And while that's. happening, we also animate the shader. itself. We use gap to animate the. progress uniform from 0 to one over a. couple seconds with a nice easing. This. drives the distortion effect inside the. fragment shader. So while the content is. sliding out and the new one is coming. in, the image beneath also gets. distorted and transitions to the next. one. Once the shader animation. completes, we reset progress uniform. back to zero and update texture one to. hold the new current image. So we are. ready for the next transition. Now we'll. add a resize handler just to make the. canvas responsive. In handle resize. function, we update the renderer size. and also refresh the resolution uniform. so the shader knows the new viewport. dimensions. Finally, we hook everything up on window. load. We call setup initial slide to. animate the first text in and then. initialize renderer to boot up the. canvas and load the textures. We also add a click listener to trigger. the slide change and a resize listener. to keep things fluid. And that's it. The whole setup is done. We have got a full screen content slider. animated with GSAP and powered by custom.

shaders in 3JS. Hope you found the video. helpful. See you in the next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7TcfWFh4ATA

Learn how to build a responsive animated content slider using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Three.js, custom shaders, and GSAP—with liquid lens distortion and smooth text transitions.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Oscar Pico

Website: https://www.oscarpico.es/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Thanks for watching!
item.content:encoded:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 深入了解如何使用 Three.js、自訂 Shaders 和 GSAP 共同打造引人注目的網頁動畫效果，特別是帶有鏡頭扭曲的內容滑塊。
  • 學習如何將複雜的專案分解為獨立的部分：HTML 結構、CSS 樣式、資料管理、Shader 編寫（即使沒有背景，也可藉助工具）和 JavaScript 邏輯。
  • 探索如何準備和控制文字元素的動畫（例如使用 GSAP SplitText 插件），以實現精確的進場和離場效果。
  • 瞭解如何設定 WebGL 環境並將自訂 Shader 應用於紋理過渡動畫。

摘要

作者在尋找值得學習的精彩網頁動畫時，被一個網站上帶有鏡頭扭曲效果的內容滑塊深深吸引。儘管過去他傾向於避免使用 Shaders，但對這種獨特扭曲效果的好奇心促使他決定嘗試自己重建。他投入了大約八小時的時間進行嘗試，並成功建立了一個基本版本的滑塊，該版本具有相似的失真過渡效果，雖然不及原作精緻，但他認為這個過程值得分享。

因此，在本次影片中，作者將詳細分解如何使用 Three.js、自訂 Shaders 和 GSAP 來建立這種動畫內容滑塊的整個流程。

設定基礎結構與樣式

作者首先設定了基礎的 HTML 結構，力求簡潔。他創建了一個主要的容器 `slider`，內部包含一個用於 WebGL 扭曲過渡的 canvas 元素，以及一個疊加在 canvas 上方、用於顯示文字內容的 `slider-content` 容器。

`slider-content` 中包含了標題 (`h1`)、描述 (`p`) 和額外的元資訊（類型、領域、日期）。作者強調這是一個非常基礎的結構，所有動態內容和效果都將由 JavaScript 和 Shaders 控制。

接著，作者設定了 CSS 樣式。他從基本的樣式重設和字體導入開始，然後設定了 `slider` 容器為全視口大小、相對定位並隱藏溢出。Canvas 被設定為全寬高區塊元素，以確保填滿空間。

`slider-content` 則使用絕對定位疊加在 canvas 上方，並透過 z-index 確保其顯示在最上層。作者調整了標題的居中和描述的位置樣式，並使用了 Flexbox 來排列描述中的詳細資訊。

一個關鍵的部分是為 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件準備的實用工具樣式。作者為標題（字元）和描述（行）的文字元素設定了特定的 display 和 overflow 屬性，以便能精確地動畫每個字元和行，確保動畫乾淨流暢。

內容資料與 Shaders

為了保持程式碼整潔，作者將幻燈片內容儲存在一個獨立的 `slides.js` 檔案中，這是一個包含標題、描述、圖片路徑等資訊的物件陣列，這種方式方便管理和未來的動態載入。

Shaders 的部分也放在另一個 `shaders.js` 檔案中。作者說明 Vertex Shader 比較簡單，主要負責傳遞 UV 座標，而所有的視覺魔法都發生在 Fragment Shader 中。

作者坦承自己沒有編寫 Shader 的背景，並提到這個 Fragment Shader 是在 Claude AI 的幫助下完成的，用於實現影片開頭提到的圓形鏡頭扭曲過渡效果。

這個 Shader 處理當前和下一張幻燈片的圖片紋理，使用一個逐漸擴大的圓形遮罩進行過渡，並對新進來的圖片應用柔和的鏡頭扭曲。它還包含一個 `coverUV` 函式來幫助圖片正確地覆蓋視口，並暴露一個名為 `progress` 的 uniform 變數，這個變數將透過 GSAP 動畫來控制整個過渡效果的進度。

JavaScript 主邏輯：設定與文字處理

在主要的 JavaScript 檔案中，作者首先匯入了前面建立的幻燈片資料、Shaders 程式碼，以及核心庫 Three.js 和 GSAP (包括 SplitText 插件)。

他註冊了 SplitText 插件並設定了一些全域變數來管理應用程式的狀態，例如當前幻燈片索引、一個用於防止重複過渡的旗標，以及儲存已載入紋理和 Shader 材質的變數。

接著，作者建立了一些文字處理工具函式。`createCharacterElements` 函式手動將標題中的每個字元包裹在多個 span 標籤中，以便能夠精確地控制每一個字元的動畫。

`createLineElements` 函式則利用 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件來將段落分割成行，這使得可以輕鬆地動畫整行的文字。

最後，一個名為 `processTextElements` 的輔助函式整合了前兩者，這個函式會找到 H1 標題並應用字元分割，然後找到描述中的所有段落並應用行分割，這樣在載入新幻燈片時只需呼叫這個函式就能準備好所有文字元素進行動畫。

幻燈片元素建立

作者開始說明如何處理實際的幻燈片內容的創建和動態插入。他介紹了一個函式 `createSlideElement`，這個函式接收特定幻燈片的資料（標題、描述等），並返回一個結構完整的 DOM 元素，代表該幻燈片的內容區塊。

這個函式會動態創建一個帶有 `slider-content` 類的 div，並在其中注入根據資料生成的 HTML 結構，包括標題、描述和元資訊。初始創建時，這個元素的透明度會被設為零，以便稍後通過動畫使其淡入。

================================================

For a while now, this site has been. sitting on my list of must do breakdowns. as it's packed with some incredible. animations. Among everything it. showcases, what really stood out to me. was this sleek content slider. As you. scroll, the images shift with a striking. lens distortion effect. While the. content glides out and the next one. flows in, super smooth and visually. captivating. What made this especially. challenging was this distortion. animation. I would never built anything. quite like it. A few years back, I was. actively avoiding shaders, but the. curiosity about how these warped effects. work never really left me. Lately. though, we have started exploring that. space more here on the channel. So, I. figured this was the perfect time to. finally try building it myself. So, I. gave it a shot and after hours of. tinkering, I managed to put together a. basic version of the slider. It triggers. on click and carries a similar. distortion transition. Not exactly the. same, but pretty close. It's not as. polished as the original, but. considering it only took about 8 hours. to rebuild, I figured it was worth. sharing the full breakdown. So, in. today's video, we'll walk through how to. build this kind of animated content. slider using 3JS, custom shaders, and. GAP. If you enjoy seeing award-winning. animations broken down and rebuilt from. scratch, make sure to like the video and. subscribe to the channel. And if you. want to access the source code for this. project, plus hundreds of other similar. micro projects and a brand new full. website template every month, you can. check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's get into it. Let's start by setting up the HTML. structure. We'll keep it minimal, just a. full screen slider wrapper that holds a. canvas for the distortion transition and. a content container layered on top of. it. So, I'll create a slider div first. This will be our main wrapper. Inside. that we'll add a canvas tag. We'll use. this later for the WebGL shader. animation. Then right after that, I'll. drop in a slider content div. This is. where we'll display the text content for. each slide like the title and. description. Inside the content, we'll have a slide. title with an H1 and a slide description. that includes a paragraph and some meta. info, type, field, and date. That's all. we need here, just a clean base.

structure. Everything else will be. driven through JavaScript and shaders. Let's move on to the styling. First, I've imported the inter font from. Google fonts. Then we'll start by. resetting the basic styles, removing. default margin and padding and setting. box sizing to border box for. consistency. For the body, I'll apply. the inter font. Now, for the headings,. we'll style the H1 to be all uppercase. Give it a bold weight and scale it up. with a flute font size that adapts to. the view port. We'll also tighten the. line height for a sharper look. paragraphs get a slightly smaller size. to keep them clean and readable. Next, I'll style the slider container. It's going to be full view port width. and height position relative with. overflow hidden and white text by. default. Inside that, the canvas will be full. width and height and set to block. So,. it fills the space without any scroll. bars. Now the content that sits on top. of the canvas will target the slider. content and absolutely position it to. cover the full area. We'll also prevent. user selection and bump its C index up. so it stays above the canvas. The slide title will be centered both. vertically and horizontally using a. transform translate and we'll align the. text in the center. Then for the description block, I'll. move it slightly below the title. Offset it a bit to one side and give it. a fixed width. I'll use flex direction column and space. out the children using a nice gap. For the extra meta info, I'll transform. the text to uppercase to match the. editorial wipe. Now comes the important. part for animations. Since we'll be. using Gap's split text plug-in to break. down the title into characters and the. paragraph into lines, I'll add some. utility styles for that. So, for the. title, I'll make sure each word is. displayed as a flex row and each. character block is set to block display. Then, for both characters and lines,. I'll hide overflow so we can animate. them cleanly in and out. And finally,. the spans inside those characters and. lines, we'll set them to inline block. enable will change on transform for. performance and make sure they're. positioned relative so we can animate. them smoothly. Lastly, I'll throw in a. quick media query to adjust the layout. on smaller screens. We'll tweak the. title and description positions and.

widen the content for better legibility. on mobile. And that's it for the. styling. Now, I have created a separate file. called slides.js just to keep things. clean. In here, I set up an array of. objects. Each one represents a slide. It. includes a title, a description, type,. field, date, and an image path. Keeping. the content separate like this makes it. super easy to manage later, especially. if you want to pull in data dynamically. or hook it up with the CMS down the. line. Now, let's talk about the shaders. There is another file called shaders.js,. which holds both vortex and fragment. shaders. These are just exported strings. that we'll inject into 3JS when setting. up our shader material. The vortex. shader is super simple. It just passes. the UV coordinates through so we can use. them in the fragment shader. Nothing. fancy here. But the fragment shader,. that's where all the magic happens. And. I mentioned this many times before. I. don't have any background in writing. shaders. So for this part, I got some. help from Claude. I basically explained. the visual effect I wanted something. like a circular lens distortion that. grows during the transition. And Claude. helped me write a GLSL shader based on. that. The shader takes two textures. our. current slide image and the next one. Then it uses circular mask to animate. the transition. While the circle grows,. it applies this soft lens distortion on. the incoming image to give that liquid. warped feel. There is also a function in. there that helps us scale and crop the. images properly so they always cover the. viewport without getting stretched. That's handled with a custom cover UV. function. And finally, I have exposed a. progress uniform that will animate with. gap to control the whole effect frame by. frame. We'll pass all these uniforms. through 3JS and update them on every. transition. All right, now that we have. got content and shaders out of the way,. let's wire things up in the main script. file. First, I'll import the data and shaders. So, we'll bring in the slides array from. the slides JS file. And then we'll. import both the vortex shader and. fragment shader from our shaders file. After that, we bring in 3JS which we'll. use to handle all the WebGL rendering. and apply custom shaders. Then we import. gap and also it split text plug-in. We'll be using this plug-in to break. down the title into individual. characters and the paragraph into.

separate lines so we can animate them. one by one. Now, right after the. imports, I'll register the split text. plug-in and turn off null target. warnings. That's just to clean up the. console a bit since GP might. occasionally target things that don't. exist yet, especially during. transitions. Then I'll set up a few. global variables to manage state. We. have got current slide index which keeps. track of which slide is currently active. as transitioning is a flag we'll use to. avoid triggering new transitions while. an animation is already playing. We'll. also store all the loaded textures in. slide textures and declared shader. material and renderer so we can. initialize them later and still access. them throughout the script. All right,. now let's handle the text splitting. We'll start with a utility function. called create character elements. This. takes a DOM element like our title and. manually wraps each character in span. tags. We are doing this because we want. the full control over every single. letters animation. So inside this. function we first check if the element. already has character elements. If it. does we return early so we don't double. wrap anything. Then we split the text. into words and for each word we create a. wpper div with the class word. Inside. that we loop through every character and. wrap it in its own character div. and inside that a span. That span is. what we'll animate later using GAP. We. also add an empty space character. between words so spacing looks natural. Now for line splitting, we have got. another function called create line. elements. This one uses Gap's split text. plug-in directly to break a paragraph. into lines and then wraps each line in a. span. Again, this lets us animate full lines. in and out with clean vertical motion. And finally, we wrap those two into an. helper function called process text. elements. This looks for the H1 title and runs it. through the character splitter. Then. finds all the paragraph tags in the. description and runs those through the. line splitter. This way whenever we load. a new slide, we can just call this one. function to prepare all the text for. animation. All right, now that we have. got text splitting utilities out of the. way, let's move on and handle how the. actual slide content gets created and. animated. We'll start with a function. called create slide element. This takes.

in the slide data like title,. description and so on and returns a. fully structured DOM element. So first. I'm creating a new div and giving it the. class slider content. I am setting its. opacity to zero for now. We'll fade it. in later as part of the animation. Then. inside this element I'm injecting the. full HTML layout for the slide that. includes the title inside an H1, the. main description inside a paragraph tag. and then extra details type field and. date inside their own container. This. structure is the same as what we had in. our HTML file earlier. It just gets. dynamically created. Now, every time we. switch slides, once that's returned, we. can append it to the tom whenever we. need a new slide. Now, let's look at how. we handle the actual animation between. slides. So, here is our animate slide. transition function. It takes in the. index of the next slide we want to show. First, I grab the current content. element and also the main slider. wrapper. Then, I create a gap timeline. We'll use this to sequence everything in. order. The first step is to animate out. the current content. So we target all. the character spans from the title and. slide them upward by animating the Y-. value. We give them a slight stagger so. the letters flow out one after another. Right after that, we do the same thing. for the paragraph lines. Again, sliding. them up with a bit of delay and a smooth. easing. Now once those are out of the. way, we run a call function inside the. timeline. This lets us inject custom. logic into the animation sequence. Inside that block, we create the next. content element using the create slide. element function we wrote earlier. Then. we kill the old timeline to stop any. leftover animations. Remove the old. slide from the DOM and append the new. one in its place. Next, we prepare the. new content for animation. We set all. these spans inside it to be pushed down. so they are hidden initially. We give it. a short delay just to ensure everything. is in place and then run process text. elements function to break the new title. and description into animatable chunks. just like before. Then we grab all the. new character and spans and animate them. into view, sliding everything up from. below. Once the animation is done, we. reset the is transitioning flag and. update the current slide index so we. know which slide is currently active. So. that's the full transition from fading. out the old content, loading the new. one, breaking it into pieces, and.

sliding everything back in. Next, we'll. look at how to set up the initial slide. and wire up the renderer. We'll start with the setup initial slide. function. This one just runs when the. page loads and it animates the very. first slide into view. So first I grab. the slider content element from the DOM. Then we call our process text elements. helper to split the title into. characters and the description into. lines just like we have been doing. during transitions. Next, we grab all. the individual spans for characters and. lines and then we animate them in using. gap. The characters comes up from below. with a bit of stagger for that fluid. motion. and the lines follow shortly after with. a small delay. This gives the first. light that smooth staggered entrance as. soon as the page loads. Now let's look at the main renderer. setup. We are creating a function called. initialize renderer. This is where all. the 3JS and shader logic kicks in. First, we create a new 3JS scene and a. basic orthographic camera. We don't need. perspective here since we are just doing. flat full screen rendering. Then, we. initialize the renderer targeting the. canvas element we placed in our HTML. earlier. I'm enabling anti-aliasing to smooth out. the edges a bit. We also set the render. size to match the current window size. Now, here comes the important part,. setting up the shader material. We are. creating a new shader material and. passing in all the uniforms we need for. our distortion effect. So we have got. uniform texture one and uniform texture. 2 for the current and next images,. uniform progress to animate the. distortion, uniform resolution to keep. track of the viewport size and the size. of both textures so we can scale them. properly inside the shader. Then we add a full screen plane mesh to. the scene using the shader material. This will render our distortion effect. directly to the canvas. Now let's load the textures. I'm using. texture loader to load all the slide. images from our array. For each one, I'm storing it in an array. and also saving the original image. dimensions so we can pass them to the. shader later. Once all the textures are loaded, we. assign the first two, slide one and. slide two as the default images for. uniform texture one and uniform texture. 2. This sets up the initial state of the.

canvas. Finally, we start the render loop. I'll. define a render function that just calls. itself with request animation frame and. renders the scene with the camera on. each frame. And that's it. Our WebGL. canvas is ready. The distortion shader. is running and we are good to trigger. transitions. Next, we'll wire up the. click event and the transition trigger. We'll start with the handle slide change. function. This gets triggered every time. we click on the page. The first thing we. do is check if a transition is already. in progress. If it is, we return early. and ignore the click just to avoid. overlapping animations. Then we set it. transitioning flag to true so nothing. else can interrupt while the transition. is running. Next, we calculate the next. slide index. So we simply increment the. current slide index and wrap around. using modulo. This gives us an endless. loop of slides. Now we update the shader. uniforms. We assign the current and next. image textures to texture one and. texture two. And we also update the size. values for both textures so the shader. can scale them correctly. Once that's. done, we call the animate slide. transition function we wrote earlier. This will handle animating the text. content between slides. And while that's. happening, we also animate the shader. itself. We use gap to animate the. progress uniform from 0 to one over a. couple seconds with a nice easing. This. drives the distortion effect inside the. fragment shader. So while the content is. sliding out and the new one is coming. in, the image beneath also gets. distorted and transitions to the next. one. Once the shader animation. completes, we reset progress uniform. back to zero and update texture one to. hold the new current image. So we are. ready for the next transition. Now we'll. add a resize handler just to make the. canvas responsive. In handle resize. function, we update the renderer size. and also refresh the resolution uniform. so the shader knows the new viewport. dimensions. Finally, we hook everything up on window. load. We call setup initial slide to. animate the first text in and then. initialize renderer to boot up the. canvas and load the textures. We also add a click listener to trigger. the slide change and a resize listener. to keep things fluid. And that's it. The whole setup is done. We have got a full screen content slider. animated with GSAP and powered by custom.

shaders in 3JS. Hope you found the video. helpful. See you in the next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7TcfWFh4ATA

Learn how to build a responsive animated content slider using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Three.js, custom shaders, and GSAP—with liquid lens distortion and smooth text transitions.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Oscar Pico

Website: https://www.oscarpico.es/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!

item.title: Scroll-Animated Cards That Escape First, Then Flip Back Mid-Scroll (GSAP & ScrollTrigger)
item.itunes:title: Scroll-Animated Cards That Escape First, Then Flip Back Mid-Scroll (GSAP & ScrollTrigger)
item.itunes:author: Codegrid
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 本摘要介紹了一個複雜且視覺效果出色的網頁卡片動畫的實現過程。
  • 它闡述了如何利用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 這兩個強大的 JavaScript 函式庫來創建基於滾動的動畫。
  • 摘要詳細說明了實現此效果所需的 HTML 結構設定（包括克服佈局挑戰的技巧）及 CSS 樣式應用。

摘要

影片的作者在瀏覽網頁設計獎項尋找靈感時，發現了一個作品集網站上令人印象深刻的動畫效果。這個效果展示了卡片如何隨著滾動而滑出、在頁面後續部分重新平滑地浮入、堆疊、然後散開並翻轉以顯示背面內容。作者對這個概念深感著迷。

技術與準備

作者決定親自嘗試重現這個效果，經過約 6 小時的實驗，他成功地使用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 實現了一個非常相似的概念。這個影片的目的，便是要逐步展示如何利用 HTML、CSS、JavaScript、GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 來建構這個精確的滾動動畫。

HTML結構 - 基本設定

作者首先開始建立基本的 HTML 結構，包括一個簡單的導覽列（nav）。接著建立主要視覺區 (hero section)，用於放置動畫開始時會滑出的第一組卡片。這些卡片此時只包含正面。隨後加入一個填充區段 (filler section) 作為簡單的間隔。

HTML結構 - 卡片重新進入技巧

實現卡片重新進入和動畫的部分是關鍵。作者解釋說，如果直接將卡片放在預定的區段，會遇到滾動觸發和固定定位帶來的佈局問題。為了解決這個問題並重現原始動畫的時間點，作者採用了一個巧妙的方案：將一個邏輯上的區段拆分為兩個實體區段。第一個是服務區段 (services section)，只包含標題；第二個是一個獨立的卡片區段 (card section)，它將在整個過程中固定在視口中，並放置在內容圖層的後方。服務區段將設定為透明，以便在滾動時能看到下方的卡片區段。之後會使用 ScrollTrigger 在正確的時間點取消固定卡片區段，創造出一個無縫的整體區段的錯覺。

HTML結構 - 完整卡片與包裹元素

接著作者建構了實際的卡片結構，這些卡片位於固定的卡片區段內。每張卡片都有一個包裹容器 (card wrapper)，內部再包含一個用於翻轉的包裹 (flip card inner)。作者強調這些多層的包裹元素非常重要，它們允許獨立應用不同的變換效果，例如浮動和翻轉，避免動畫之間的干擾。每個內部卡片又分為正面 (flip card front) 和背面 (flip card back)。正面的內容與主要視覺區的卡片保持一致以確保視覺匹配，背面則加入其他內容。最後，頁面底部加入一個結尾區段 (outro section) 完成整個 HTML 結構。

CSS樣式 - 起始設定

進入 CSS 樣式部分。作者先導入了所需的字體，並定義了一些 CSS 變數用於顏色管理。隨後進行了預設樣式重設，移除 margin 和 padding，並設定 box-sizing。

CSS樣式 - 基礎與導覽列

為 body 元素設定了基礎字體樣式，確保整體排版簡潔。接著設定了標題 (H1) 和段落的樣式，並為 span 元素應用特定的標籤樣式（大寫、特定字體、小尺寸）。導覽列 (nav) 被設定為固定在視口頂部並使用 flexbox 進行內部元素的排列。

CSS樣式 - 區段佈局

作者為所有區段定義了基礎樣式，使其佔滿螢幕寬高，並設定內邊距和隱藏溢出。主要視覺區 (hero section) 設定了淺色背景和深色文字，而 about 和 outro 區段則使用深色背景和淺色文字，並使用 flexbox 將內容水平和垂直置中。

CSS樣式 - 主要視覺區卡片

樣式化主要視覺區的卡片容器 (hero cards container)，使用絕對定位並通過 transform 將其置中。容器內部使用 flexbox 水平排列卡片並加入間距。每個卡片設定固定的長寬比、內邊距和圓角，並設定為垂直方向的 flex 容器。卡片內的標題也使用 flexbox 排列。個別的卡片則根據設計需求賦予獨特的背景顏色，並調整 Z-index 和設定變換原點，以便後續動畫控制。

CSS樣式 - 服務與固定卡片區段

為服務區段 (services section) 添加額外的內邊距，並將其標題使用 transform 推到下方，為滾動動畫預留空間。接著樣式化固定在螢幕中的卡片區段 (card section)，將其延伸至全寬高，並通過負 Z-index 使其位於其他元素下方。背景顏色設定為淺色，以匹配主要視覺區。

CSS樣式 - 完整卡片細節

樣式化卡片區段內的卡片容器 (cards container)，使用 flexbox 將其內部內容置中並加入卡片間距和頂部外邊距。該區段內的每個卡片同樣設定長寬比，並添加 3D 透視效果，以便後續應用 3D 翻轉動畫。

CSS樣式 - 包裹元素與翻轉準備

樣式化卡片內部最重要的包裹元素：card wrapper 用於使用絕對定位和 translate 技巧置中內部內容，同時這個包裹層將應用一個簡單的 keyframe 浮動動畫，使其輕微上下移動。flip card inner 包裹層則啟用 preserve-3d 屬性，確保卡片正反面能在 3D 空間中正確翻轉。

CSS樣式 - 卡片正反面

樣式化 flip card front 和 flip card back。兩者都被設定為絕對定位、佔滿父容器空間，並隱藏背面可見性 (backface-visibility: hidden)。它們都有圓角並隱藏溢出。正面設定內邊距並使用 flexbox 垂直排列內容，背景顏色與主要視覺區對應的卡片顏色一致。背面則被初始旋轉 180 度，以便在翻轉時能正確顯示。

================================================

While browsing awards for inspiration, I. came across a portfolio site that had. just one site of the day. Right away, I. was impressed by how polished everything. felt. From the design direction to the. animations and interactions. Out of all. the interactions, this one stood out. The hero cards slide outward as you. scroll, then reappear in a later. section, but not in a typical way. As. you keep scrolling, the cards float. smoothly back into view and settle into. a neat stack. Then with more scroll they. spread across the screen, flip around. and reveal their backsides all while. maintaining the smooth floating motion. throughout. I found the concept. fascinating. So I gave it a shot myself. After about 6 hours of experimenting, I. managed to recreate a very similar. concept using Gap and scroll trigger. You can see everything is there from the. card slide out at the start to the. floating return to the flipping. animation that reveals the content. In. today's video, I'll show you how you can. build this exact scroll animation using. HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and scroll. trigger. If you like seeing such. award-winning web animations broken down. and rebuilt from scratch, give this. video a like and subscribe to the. channel if you haven't already. And if. you want to access the source code for. this project, plus hundreds of other. similar micro projects and a brand new. full website template every month, you. can check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's get into it. Let's start by. setting up the HTML structure. Beginning. with a simple nav bar. I'll add just two. elements inside it for now. One for the. logo and one for the menu button each. wrapped in a span with placeholder text. This part is mostly for visual balance. So feel free to skip it if your focus is. purely on the animation. Next, let's. create the hero section. Inside it, I'll. add a container called hero cards. This. is where we'll place the first set of. cards. Now the idea here is to use these. cards just for the initial slide out. animation. So we'll only add the front. face of each card. Later in the page,. we'll create the full versions of these. cards with both front and back sides. which will slide in from the top to give. the illusion that the same cards are. re-entering in a new section. In. reality, both versions will be separate. elements, but by making their front. faces look identical, we can pull off. that seamless visual trick. So for now. I'll create a div with the class card. and assign it a unique ID. Inside that. I'll add two wrappers with the class. card title and use pen text to show the. card label and index. I'm wrapping them. in divs so we can easily lay them out. using flexbox later. Next I'll duplicate. this card two more times. Change their. ids and update the content accordingly. Giving us three cards in total for the.

hero section. With that done, the hero. cards are ready. Now let's add a simple filler section. below which I'll call about. This will. just have an H1 with some placeholder. text on a black background. Nothing. functional, just for spacing. Then comes. the key part, the section where we want. the cards to re-enter and animate. I'll. call this section services. Here I'll. add a section header div and drop an H1. inside with some placeholder text. Now. technically this is where our full cards. should appear. But if you look at the. original animation, the cards actually. begin sliding in when this section is. already halfway in view. To recreate the. timing, I first tried placing the cards. directly inside this section, but it. didn't work out because I needed to. animate based on scroll progress and. pinning midscroll caused layout issues. If I set the scroll trigger start point. towardly, the pinning happens midway,. which isn't what we want. And if I set. it to start right at the top, then we. lose the entry timing. So the solution I. landed on is to split it into two. sections. The first is the services. section which only holds the header. Then I'll create a separate card section. which will remain fixed in the viewport. the entire time behind the content. layers. We'll make the services section. transparent so the card section appears. visible underneath as the user scrolls. Later using scroll trigger we'll unpin. the card section at the right moment. Just after the services section finishes. spinning to create the illusion that. it's all in one seamless section. Now. let's build the actual cards inside the. card section I'll start by adding a. container called cards container to hold. everything. Then just like before I'll. add a card div with a unique id inside. that I'll create a card wrapper div. which will act as a holder for the. internal content. Then within that I'll. add a div with the class flip cardainer. All of these wrappers are important. because they let us separate different. transformations for floating and. flipping. So, one animation doesn't. interfere with another. You'll see in. the JavaScript later that we use each. rapper to apply specific transforms,. letting us animate position, scale, and. rotation independently. Now, let's split. the inner card into two parts. Flip card. front and flip card back. On the front. side, I'll add the same content we used. in the hero cards, so the two versions. visually match. For the back side, I add. some placeholder content as well. And in. the middle, insert a card copy div. Inside that, I'll list out the service. items using paragraph elements.
