That's. essentially the full HTML structure for. one card. I'll go ahead and duplicate it. twice, change the IDs and the content,. and we'll have all three cards ready. Finally, I'll add an outro section at. the very end with a simple H1 and some.

placeholder text to close things out. And that's the full HTML structure done. Let's move on to the styling. You can see I've already imported two. fonts from Google Fonts. One is DM Sense. and the other is DM Mono. Next, I have. defined a few CSS variables for colors. This includes a dark shade, a couple of. light neutrals, and three accent colors,. one for each card. Then I'll reset the. default styling using the universal. selector, removing margin and padding,. and setting box sizing to border box. For the body, I'll apply the DMs font to. keep the overall typography clean and. modern. Now, for the heading styles,. I'll keep H1 slightly bold with a medium. large size and give paragraph elements a. comfortable size with a bit of weight as. well. For all the span elements, I'll. make the text uppercase, apply the mono. font, and set a smaller size with medium. weight to give it that label-like look. Moving on to the nav, I'll fix it at the. top of the screen, make it stretch full. width, and add some padding. Then I'll. use flexbox to space out the logo and. the menu button. Both the logo and menu span elements. will get some padding and a slight. border radius. The logo gets a dark background with. light text and the menu gets the. opposite light background with dark. text. Next, I'll define some base styles for. every section. Each section will be set. to take full width and height of the. screen with a bit of padding and. overflow hidden to help with the. animations. For the hero section, I'll. set the background to a light color and. the text to dark for good contrast. The about and out sections will use a. dark background with light text. And. I'll center their content both. vertically and horizontal using flexbox. Now let's style the hero cards. container. I'll absolutely position it. in the center of the screen using top. left and transform and give it a fixed. width. I'll use flexbox to align the. cards horizontally with some spacing in. between. Each card inside hero cards will have a. fixed aspect ratio, padding, and border. radius. I'll also set them to be column flex. containers spaced from top to bottom. The card title inside each card will be. styled as a horizontal flex row with. space between the span elements. The. span themselves will get a smaller font. size to give that minimalist design. feel. For the individual hero cards,. I'll assign them their unique accent. background colors. I'll also adjust. their Zindex and set transform origin. for the left and right cards to control. how they animate later. Now for each services section, I'll give. it extra padding to leave enough room. for scroll interaction. Inside it, the. services header will be positioned.

relative, center aligned, and pushed way. down using a transform. This will allow. us to animate it into view using scroll. later. I also will change so the browser. can optimize that animation. Now we'll style the card section. This. one is fixed to the screen and stretched. full width and height sitting behind. everything else using a negative Z. index. The background is set to light to match. the hero section. Inside it, the cards container will be. centered using flexbox with some spacing. between the cards and a bit of top. margin so they don't sit too high. Each card in this section will again. follow the same aspect ratio. I'm also. adding a 3D perspective here so we can. apply the flip animations in 3D space. Now for the card wrapper, this is where. I'll center the inner content using. absolute positioning and a translate. trick. I'll also apply floating animation to. this wrapper. It's a simple key frame animation that. gently moves the card up and down on a. loop to offset the motion slightly for. each card. I'll add a delay to the. animation for second and third cards. Next, the flip card inner will have. preserve 3D enabled so that both front. and back faces can flipped in 3D without. flattening. Flip card front and flip card back will. be absolutely positioned, take full size. and have hidden back faces. I'm also giving them a border radius and. hiding overflow. The front side will have some padding. and use flexbox to space the titles. vertically. The background colors here will match. the same accent colors as their hero. counterparts. For the back face, I'll rotate it by. 180° so it flips into view when we. trigger the rotation later. It gets the. same padding and layout, but the. background is set to white. I've also. added a bit of spacing between its. elements using a gap. Inside the back face, the card copy. container will be a vertical stack with. a small gap between each item. Each paragraph inside will be centered. with a readable font size, a light. background, and slightly rounded. corners. Now I'll define the initial transforms. for each of the three cards. Card one is. pushed off to the right and slightly. rotated. Guard two sits in the middle. with no rotation. Guard three is pushed. off to the left with a slight positive. rotation. I'm also scaling all three. down so they animate up into full size. later. Finally, I'll set the default. opacity of all cards to zero so they. fade in as we scroll. And that wraps up. the CSS part. Next, we'll move into the. animation part using JavaScript and Gab. Let's start by setting up the basics.

First, I'm importing Gap along with the. scroll trigger plugin and Lennis, which. we'll be using to add smooth scrolling. Once the page finishes loading, I'll. register scroll trigger so we can start. using it in our animations. Then I'll initialize Lennis and connect. it to scroll trigger. This makes sure. that every time we scroll with Lennis,. scroll trigger is aware of the scroll. position and updates accordingly. To. keep everything in sync, I'll also hook. Lennis into Gap's internal animation. loop. This ensures the scroll position. gets updated on every frame, giving a. super smooth performance. Next, I define. a small helper function. This function. smooths out the progress values we get. from scroll trigger, easing them in and. out to make our animations feel more. natural. We'll use this smoothing. technique throughout the rest of the. code. Now, let's animate the hero cards. as user scrolls. I'm creating a scroll. trigger that watches the hero section. The animation starts when the top of the. hero section hits the top of the screen. and ends when about 3/4 of that section. has scrolled past. I have enabled. scrubbing here which means the animation. will play in sync with the scroll. progress. The slower you scroll, the. slower the animation runs and vice. versa. Inside the onupdate call back, I. first grab the progress value. This will. be a number between 0 and one. representing how far we have scrolled. through the animation range. Then I use. that progress to slowly fade out the. entire hero cards container. The opacity. starts at full and fades to about half. by the time the section scrolls out. Next, I animate each of the three cards. individually. I loop through them one by. one and give each card a slight delay so. that animations feel staggered. To. calculate how much each card should. move, I take the scroll progress and. offset it slightly based on the card's. index. This gives us a unique progress. value for each card so they don't. animate at the exact same time. Then I. animate their vertical movement. As you. scroll, each card moves downwards. starting from its original position and. floating all the way down off screen. At. the same time, I scale each card down. starting at its original size and. gradually shrinking it to about 3/4. Now, for the horizontal movement and. rotation, this is where we separate the. cards visually. The middle card stays. centered with no rotation, but the first. card moves to the right and rotates. slightly counterclockwise, while the. third card moves to the left and rotates. clockwise. The subtle offsets give that. fanned out sliding effect that makes the. whole interaction feel dynamic and. layered. All of these animations are. tied to scroll smoothed out using that. easing function we defined earlier which. makes the transitions feel soft and. fluid. So as the user scrolls past the.

hero section, the cards gently fade out,. float down, shift apart, and scale down. setting the stage for them to reappear. later. Now, let's set up the next part. of the scroll animation where the. services section takes over and cards. begin to re-enter. First, I'm creating a. scroll trigger on the services section. This trigger starts when the top of the. section hits the top of the screen and. runs for about four screen heights. So,. we have enough vertical space to animate. everything smoothly. I'm also pinning. the services section in place during. that range. This means as you scroll,. the section will stay fixed while the. rest of the animation plays out on top. of it. That's the moment where the cards. begin to float back in. Now, here's the. important part. You might remember how. our actual animated cards are placed in. a completely separate section called. cards. We want those cards to appear. behind the services section and move in. perfect sync with it so the user never. realizes they are from a different. section. To make that illusion work, I'm. creating another scroll trigger on the. same range. As soon as the services. section scrolls out of view, meaning. it's no longer pinned, I switch the. position of card section from fixed to. absolute, this anchors the cards in. place at the exact point where the. services section left off. I do this by. grabbing the top position of the. services section and setting the card. section to that same spot on the page. This allows the cards to scroll along. naturally with the rest of the content. as we move forward. Now when we scroll back up, I reverse. that. I set the card section back to. fixed so it's ready to play the. animation again in reverse. This back. and forth positioning lets us animate. the cards freely while keeping them. visually locked to the services section. even though technically they are in. different layer behind it. Now we are. setting up the most important part. The. scroll animation that brings the card. back into view, spreads them out and. flips them to reveal their content. We. are creating a scroll trigger on the. services section. The animation starts. when the section just enters the bottom. of the screen and it runs for a distance. equal to four full screen heights. Enough room to stretch out and control. each movement gradually. Scrubbing is. enabled here which means the animation. is directly linked to scroll. Plays. forward as you scroll down and reverses. as you scroll back up. Every pixel of. scroll controls the timing. Inside the. on-update call back, we grab the scroll. progress, a value that goes from 0 to. one. Depending on how far we have. scrolled through that triggers range. The first thing we animate is the header. inside the services section. It starts. way below its original position, offset. by 400%, which means it's pushed down by.

four times its height. As you scroll, we. ease that value back down to 0%,. bringing the header into view from. below. It moves smoothly because we are. applying that easing function we defined. earlier. Now we move on to animating the. three cards. We loop through each card. and introduce a slight delay for each. one using its index. This delay is. created by subtracting a small value. from the scroll progress based on the. index. Let's go through the transformations we. are applying to each card. First. vertical position. Each card starts. fully off screen above the viewport. offset by minus 100%. As the scroll. progresses, the car drops down to the. middle at 50%. Then finally settles into place at 0%. This gives that floating decent effect. as if the cards are drifting back down. into view. Next, scaling. We start with each card. scaled down to 25 or 25% of its full. size. As the scroll progresses, it grows to 75. and eventually scales up to one, which. is its original size. This gradual scaling helps emphasis that. the card is re-entering and growing back. into focus. Now, opacity. At the start, the opacity. is set to zero, meaning the card is. fully transparent. As soon as the scroll begins, we fade it. in, increasing opacity up to one. This. happens fairly early in the animation to. make the cards feel like they. materialize softly instead of snapping. in. Then horizontal position and. rotation. Card one is initially pushed. to the right by 100% and rotated by. minus 5°. Card 3 is pushed to the left by minus. 100% and rotated by 5°. Card two stays. centered with no rotation. As you scroll, each card gradually moves. back toward the center. The left and. right cards interpolate their X position. from plus or minus 100% back to 0% and. their rotation value is back to 0°. So. all the cards straighten out and align. neatly side by side. And finally the. flip. This is where the back side of the. card gets revealed. We rotate the card. around its Y-axis. That's the horizontal. axis that creates a card flipping. effect. The rotation goes from 0° to. 180° completing a full half turn. When. it hits 180, the back face is fully. visible. Each card has a flip card in a. wrapper specifically for this flip. So. we can rotate just that part without. affecting the positioning or scale. By. combining these transforms, vertical. drop, scale up, fade in, horizontal. shift, rotation flattening, and finally. the Y-axis flip, we recreate that smooth. magical movement where the cards float. back, regroup in the center, and flip. open to show their content. All of these. changes happen smoothly thanks to our. custom smooth step easing function, and.

are timed across a four screen scroll. range. This gives the animation plenty. of space to breathe and lets each face. happen gradually and clearly. Hope you. found the video helpful. See you in the. next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8MUZcfnuYvA

Learn how to create animated cards using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and ScrollTrigger — complete with scroll-driven slide outs, floating re-entry, and 3D flip effects.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Eduard Bodak

Website: https://www.eduardbodak.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.description:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 本摘要介紹了一個複雜且視覺效果出色的網頁卡片動畫的實現過程。
  • 它闡述了如何利用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 這兩個強大的 JavaScript 函式庫來創建基於滾動的動畫。
  • 摘要詳細說明了實現此效果所需的 HTML 結構設定（包括克服佈局挑戰的技巧）及 CSS 樣式應用。

摘要

影片的作者在瀏覽網頁設計獎項尋找靈感時，發現了一個作品集網站上令人印象深刻的動畫效果。這個效果展示了卡片如何隨著滾動而滑出、在頁面後續部分重新平滑地浮入、堆疊、然後散開並翻轉以顯示背面內容。作者對這個概念深感著迷。

技術與準備

作者決定親自嘗試重現這個效果，經過約 6 小時的實驗，他成功地使用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 實現了一個非常相似的概念。這個影片的目的，便是要逐步展示如何利用 HTML、CSS、JavaScript、GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 來建構這個精確的滾動動畫。

HTML結構 - 基本設定

作者首先開始建立基本的 HTML 結構，包括一個簡單的導覽列（nav）。接著建立主要視覺區 (hero section)，用於放置動畫開始時會滑出的第一組卡片。這些卡片此時只包含正面。隨後加入一個填充區段 (filler section) 作為簡單的間隔。

HTML結構 - 卡片重新進入技巧

實現卡片重新進入和動畫的部分是關鍵。作者解釋說，如果直接將卡片放在預定的區段，會遇到滾動觸發和固定定位帶來的佈局問題。為了解決這個問題並重現原始動畫的時間點，作者採用了一個巧妙的方案：將一個邏輯上的區段拆分為兩個實體區段。第一個是服務區段 (services section)，只包含標題；第二個是一個獨立的卡片區段 (card section)，它將在整個過程中固定在視口中，並放置在內容圖層的後方。服務區段將設定為透明，以便在滾動時能看到下方的卡片區段。之後會使用 ScrollTrigger 在正確的時間點取消固定卡片區段，創造出一個無縫的整體區段的錯覺。

HTML結構 - 完整卡片與包裹元素

接著作者建構了實際的卡片結構，這些卡片位於固定的卡片區段內。每張卡片都有一個包裹容器 (card wrapper)，內部再包含一個用於翻轉的包裹 (flip card inner)。作者強調這些多層的包裹元素非常重要，它們允許獨立應用不同的變換效果，例如浮動和翻轉，避免動畫之間的干擾。每個內部卡片又分為正面 (flip card front) 和背面 (flip card back)。正面的內容與主要視覺區的卡片保持一致以確保視覺匹配，背面則加入其他內容。最後，頁面底部加入一個結尾區段 (outro section) 完成整個 HTML 結構。

CSS樣式 - 起始設定

進入 CSS 樣式部分。作者先導入了所需的字體，並定義了一些 CSS 變數用於顏色管理。隨後進行了預設樣式重設，移除 margin 和 padding，並設定 box-sizing。

CSS樣式 - 基礎與導覽列

為 body 元素設定了基礎字體樣式，確保整體排版簡潔。接著設定了標題 (H1) 和段落的樣式，並為 span 元素應用特定的標籤樣式（大寫、特定字體、小尺寸）。導覽列 (nav) 被設定為固定在視口頂部並使用 flexbox 進行內部元素的排列。

CSS樣式 - 區段佈局

作者為所有區段定義了基礎樣式，使其佔滿螢幕寬高，並設定內邊距和隱藏溢出。主要視覺區 (hero section) 設定了淺色背景和深色文字，而 about 和 outro 區段則使用深色背景和淺色文字，並使用 flexbox 將內容水平和垂直置中。

CSS樣式 - 主要視覺區卡片

樣式化主要視覺區的卡片容器 (hero cards container)，使用絕對定位並通過 transform 將其置中。容器內部使用 flexbox 水平排列卡片並加入間距。每個卡片設定固定的長寬比、內邊距和圓角，並設定為垂直方向的 flex 容器。卡片內的標題也使用 flexbox 排列。個別的卡片則根據設計需求賦予獨特的背景顏色，並調整 Z-index 和設定變換原點，以便後續動畫控制。

CSS樣式 - 服務與固定卡片區段

為服務區段 (services section) 添加額外的內邊距，並將其標題使用 transform 推到下方，為滾動動畫預留空間。接著樣式化固定在螢幕中的卡片區段 (card section)，將其延伸至全寬高，並通過負 Z-index 使其位於其他元素下方。背景顏色設定為淺色，以匹配主要視覺區。

CSS樣式 - 完整卡片細節

樣式化卡片區段內的卡片容器 (cards container)，使用 flexbox 將其內部內容置中並加入卡片間距和頂部外邊距。該區段內的每個卡片同樣設定長寬比，並添加 3D 透視效果，以便後續應用 3D 翻轉動畫。

CSS樣式 - 包裹元素與翻轉準備

樣式化卡片內部最重要的包裹元素：card wrapper 用於使用絕對定位和 translate 技巧置中內部內容，同時這個包裹層將應用一個簡單的 keyframe 浮動動畫，使其輕微上下移動。flip card inner 包裹層則啟用 preserve-3d 屬性，確保卡片正反面能在 3D 空間中正確翻轉。

CSS樣式 - 卡片正反面

樣式化 flip card front 和 flip card back。兩者都被設定為絕對定位、佔滿父容器空間，並隱藏背面可見性 (backface-visibility: hidden)。它們都有圓角並隱藏溢出。正面設定內邊距並使用 flexbox 垂直排列內容，背景顏色與主要視覺區對應的卡片顏色一致。背面則被初始旋轉 180 度，以便在翻轉時能正確顯示。

================================================

While browsing awards for inspiration, I. came across a portfolio site that had. just one site of the day. Right away, I. was impressed by how polished everything. felt. From the design direction to the. animations and interactions. Out of all. the interactions, this one stood out. The hero cards slide outward as you. scroll, then reappear in a later. section, but not in a typical way. As. you keep scrolling, the cards float. smoothly back into view and settle into. a neat stack. Then with more scroll they. spread across the screen, flip around. and reveal their backsides all while. maintaining the smooth floating motion. throughout. I found the concept. fascinating. So I gave it a shot myself. After about 6 hours of experimenting, I. managed to recreate a very similar. concept using Gap and scroll trigger. You can see everything is there from the. card slide out at the start to the. floating return to the flipping. animation that reveals the content. In. today's video, I'll show you how you can. build this exact scroll animation using. HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and scroll. trigger. If you like seeing such. award-winning web animations broken down. and rebuilt from scratch, give this. video a like and subscribe to the. channel if you haven't already. And if. you want to access the source code for. this project, plus hundreds of other. similar micro projects and a brand new. full website template every month, you. can check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's get into it. Let's start by. setting up the HTML structure. Beginning. with a simple nav bar. I'll add just two. elements inside it for now. One for the. logo and one for the menu button each. wrapped in a span with placeholder text. This part is mostly for visual balance. So feel free to skip it if your focus is. purely on the animation. Next, let's. create the hero section. Inside it, I'll. add a container called hero cards. This. is where we'll place the first set of. cards. Now the idea here is to use these. cards just for the initial slide out. animation. So we'll only add the front. face of each card. Later in the page,. we'll create the full versions of these. cards with both front and back sides. which will slide in from the top to give. the illusion that the same cards are. re-entering in a new section. In. reality, both versions will be separate. elements, but by making their front. faces look identical, we can pull off. that seamless visual trick. So for now. I'll create a div with the class card. and assign it a unique ID. Inside that. I'll add two wrappers with the class. card title and use pen text to show the. card label and index. I'm wrapping them. in divs so we can easily lay them out. using flexbox later. Next I'll duplicate. this card two more times. Change their. ids and update the content accordingly. Giving us three cards in total for the.

hero section. With that done, the hero. cards are ready. Now let's add a simple filler section. below which I'll call about. This will. just have an H1 with some placeholder. text on a black background. Nothing. functional, just for spacing. Then comes. the key part, the section where we want. the cards to re-enter and animate. I'll. call this section services. Here I'll. add a section header div and drop an H1. inside with some placeholder text. Now. technically this is where our full cards. should appear. But if you look at the. original animation, the cards actually. begin sliding in when this section is. already halfway in view. To recreate the. timing, I first tried placing the cards. directly inside this section, but it. didn't work out because I needed to. animate based on scroll progress and. pinning midscroll caused layout issues. If I set the scroll trigger start point. towardly, the pinning happens midway,. which isn't what we want. And if I set. it to start right at the top, then we. lose the entry timing. So the solution I. landed on is to split it into two. sections. The first is the services. section which only holds the header. Then I'll create a separate card section. which will remain fixed in the viewport. the entire time behind the content. layers. We'll make the services section. transparent so the card section appears. visible underneath as the user scrolls. Later using scroll trigger we'll unpin. the card section at the right moment. Just after the services section finishes. spinning to create the illusion that. it's all in one seamless section. Now. let's build the actual cards inside the. card section I'll start by adding a. container called cards container to hold. everything. Then just like before I'll. add a card div with a unique id inside. that I'll create a card wrapper div. which will act as a holder for the. internal content. Then within that I'll. add a div with the class flip cardainer. All of these wrappers are important. because they let us separate different. transformations for floating and. flipping. So, one animation doesn't. interfere with another. You'll see in. the JavaScript later that we use each. rapper to apply specific transforms,. letting us animate position, scale, and. rotation independently. Now, let's split. the inner card into two parts. Flip card. front and flip card back. On the front. side, I'll add the same content we used. in the hero cards, so the two versions. visually match. For the back side, I add. some placeholder content as well. And in. the middle, insert a card copy div. Inside that, I'll list out the service. items using paragraph elements. That's. essentially the full HTML structure for. one card. I'll go ahead and duplicate it. twice, change the IDs and the content,. and we'll have all three cards ready. Finally, I'll add an outro section at. the very end with a simple H1 and some.

placeholder text to close things out. And that's the full HTML structure done. Let's move on to the styling. You can see I've already imported two. fonts from Google Fonts. One is DM Sense. and the other is DM Mono. Next, I have. defined a few CSS variables for colors. This includes a dark shade, a couple of. light neutrals, and three accent colors,. one for each card. Then I'll reset the. default styling using the universal. selector, removing margin and padding,. and setting box sizing to border box. For the body, I'll apply the DMs font to. keep the overall typography clean and. modern. Now, for the heading styles,. I'll keep H1 slightly bold with a medium. large size and give paragraph elements a. comfortable size with a bit of weight as. well. For all the span elements, I'll. make the text uppercase, apply the mono. font, and set a smaller size with medium. weight to give it that label-like look. Moving on to the nav, I'll fix it at the. top of the screen, make it stretch full. width, and add some padding. Then I'll. use flexbox to space out the logo and. the menu button. Both the logo and menu span elements. will get some padding and a slight. border radius. The logo gets a dark background with. light text and the menu gets the. opposite light background with dark. text. Next, I'll define some base styles for. every section. Each section will be set. to take full width and height of the. screen with a bit of padding and. overflow hidden to help with the. animations. For the hero section, I'll. set the background to a light color and. the text to dark for good contrast. The about and out sections will use a. dark background with light text. And. I'll center their content both. vertically and horizontal using flexbox. Now let's style the hero cards. container. I'll absolutely position it. in the center of the screen using top. left and transform and give it a fixed. width. I'll use flexbox to align the. cards horizontally with some spacing in. between. Each card inside hero cards will have a. fixed aspect ratio, padding, and border. radius. I'll also set them to be column flex. containers spaced from top to bottom. The card title inside each card will be. styled as a horizontal flex row with. space between the span elements. The. span themselves will get a smaller font. size to give that minimalist design. feel. For the individual hero cards,. I'll assign them their unique accent. background colors. I'll also adjust. their Zindex and set transform origin. for the left and right cards to control. how they animate later. Now for each services section, I'll give. it extra padding to leave enough room. for scroll interaction. Inside it, the. services header will be positioned.

relative, center aligned, and pushed way. down using a transform. This will allow. us to animate it into view using scroll. later. I also will change so the browser. can optimize that animation. Now we'll style the card section. This. one is fixed to the screen and stretched. full width and height sitting behind. everything else using a negative Z. index. The background is set to light to match. the hero section. Inside it, the cards container will be. centered using flexbox with some spacing. between the cards and a bit of top. margin so they don't sit too high. Each card in this section will again. follow the same aspect ratio. I'm also. adding a 3D perspective here so we can. apply the flip animations in 3D space. Now for the card wrapper, this is where. I'll center the inner content using. absolute positioning and a translate. trick. I'll also apply floating animation to. this wrapper. It's a simple key frame animation that. gently moves the card up and down on a. loop to offset the motion slightly for. each card. I'll add a delay to the. animation for second and third cards. Next, the flip card inner will have. preserve 3D enabled so that both front. and back faces can flipped in 3D without. flattening. Flip card front and flip card back will. be absolutely positioned, take full size. and have hidden back faces. I'm also giving them a border radius and. hiding overflow. The front side will have some padding. and use flexbox to space the titles. vertically. The background colors here will match. the same accent colors as their hero. counterparts. For the back face, I'll rotate it by. 180° so it flips into view when we. trigger the rotation later. It gets the. same padding and layout, but the. background is set to white. I've also. added a bit of spacing between its. elements using a gap. Inside the back face, the card copy. container will be a vertical stack with. a small gap between each item. Each paragraph inside will be centered. with a readable font size, a light. background, and slightly rounded. corners. Now I'll define the initial transforms. for each of the three cards. Card one is. pushed off to the right and slightly. rotated. Guard two sits in the middle. with no rotation. Guard three is pushed. off to the left with a slight positive. rotation. I'm also scaling all three. down so they animate up into full size. later. Finally, I'll set the default. opacity of all cards to zero so they. fade in as we scroll. And that wraps up. the CSS part. Next, we'll move into the. animation part using JavaScript and Gab. Let's start by setting up the basics.

First, I'm importing Gap along with the. scroll trigger plugin and Lennis, which. we'll be using to add smooth scrolling. Once the page finishes loading, I'll. register scroll trigger so we can start. using it in our animations. Then I'll initialize Lennis and connect. it to scroll trigger. This makes sure. that every time we scroll with Lennis,. scroll trigger is aware of the scroll. position and updates accordingly. To. keep everything in sync, I'll also hook. Lennis into Gap's internal animation. loop. This ensures the scroll position. gets updated on every frame, giving a. super smooth performance. Next, I define. a small helper function. This function. smooths out the progress values we get. from scroll trigger, easing them in and. out to make our animations feel more. natural. We'll use this smoothing. technique throughout the rest of the. code. Now, let's animate the hero cards. as user scrolls. I'm creating a scroll. trigger that watches the hero section. The animation starts when the top of the. hero section hits the top of the screen. and ends when about 3/4 of that section. has scrolled past. I have enabled. scrubbing here which means the animation. will play in sync with the scroll. progress. The slower you scroll, the. slower the animation runs and vice. versa. Inside the onupdate call back, I. first grab the progress value. This will. be a number between 0 and one. representing how far we have scrolled. through the animation range. Then I use. that progress to slowly fade out the. entire hero cards container. The opacity. starts at full and fades to about half. by the time the section scrolls out. Next, I animate each of the three cards. individually. I loop through them one by. one and give each card a slight delay so. that animations feel staggered. To. calculate how much each card should. move, I take the scroll progress and. offset it slightly based on the card's. index. This gives us a unique progress. value for each card so they don't. animate at the exact same time. Then I. animate their vertical movement. As you. scroll, each card moves downwards. starting from its original position and. floating all the way down off screen. At. the same time, I scale each card down. starting at its original size and. gradually shrinking it to about 3/4. Now, for the horizontal movement and. rotation, this is where we separate the. cards visually. The middle card stays. centered with no rotation, but the first. card moves to the right and rotates. slightly counterclockwise, while the. third card moves to the left and rotates. clockwise. The subtle offsets give that. fanned out sliding effect that makes the. whole interaction feel dynamic and. layered. All of these animations are. tied to scroll smoothed out using that. easing function we defined earlier which. makes the transitions feel soft and. fluid. So as the user scrolls past the.

hero section, the cards gently fade out,. float down, shift apart, and scale down. setting the stage for them to reappear. later. Now, let's set up the next part. of the scroll animation where the. services section takes over and cards. begin to re-enter. First, I'm creating a. scroll trigger on the services section. This trigger starts when the top of the. section hits the top of the screen and. runs for about four screen heights. So,. we have enough vertical space to animate. everything smoothly. I'm also pinning. the services section in place during. that range. This means as you scroll,. the section will stay fixed while the. rest of the animation plays out on top. of it. That's the moment where the cards. begin to float back in. Now, here's the. important part. You might remember how. our actual animated cards are placed in. a completely separate section called. cards. We want those cards to appear. behind the services section and move in. perfect sync with it so the user never. realizes they are from a different. section. To make that illusion work, I'm. creating another scroll trigger on the. same range. As soon as the services. section scrolls out of view, meaning. it's no longer pinned, I switch the. position of card section from fixed to. absolute, this anchors the cards in. place at the exact point where the. services section left off. I do this by. grabbing the top position of the. services section and setting the card. section to that same spot on the page. This allows the cards to scroll along. naturally with the rest of the content. as we move forward. Now when we scroll back up, I reverse. that. I set the card section back to. fixed so it's ready to play the. animation again in reverse. This back. and forth positioning lets us animate. the cards freely while keeping them. visually locked to the services section. even though technically they are in. different layer behind it. Now we are. setting up the most important part. The. scroll animation that brings the card. back into view, spreads them out and. flips them to reveal their content. We. are creating a scroll trigger on the. services section. The animation starts. when the section just enters the bottom. of the screen and it runs for a distance. equal to four full screen heights. Enough room to stretch out and control. each movement gradually. Scrubbing is. enabled here which means the animation. is directly linked to scroll. Plays. forward as you scroll down and reverses. as you scroll back up. Every pixel of. scroll controls the timing. Inside the. on-update call back, we grab the scroll. progress, a value that goes from 0 to. one. Depending on how far we have. scrolled through that triggers range. The first thing we animate is the header. inside the services section. It starts. way below its original position, offset. by 400%, which means it's pushed down by.

four times its height. As you scroll, we. ease that value back down to 0%,. bringing the header into view from. below. It moves smoothly because we are. applying that easing function we defined. earlier. Now we move on to animating the. three cards. We loop through each card. and introduce a slight delay for each. one using its index. This delay is. created by subtracting a small value. from the scroll progress based on the. index. Let's go through the transformations we. are applying to each card. First. vertical position. Each card starts. fully off screen above the viewport. offset by minus 100%. As the scroll. progresses, the car drops down to the. middle at 50%. Then finally settles into place at 0%. This gives that floating decent effect. as if the cards are drifting back down. into view. Next, scaling. We start with each card. scaled down to 25 or 25% of its full. size. As the scroll progresses, it grows to 75. and eventually scales up to one, which. is its original size. This gradual scaling helps emphasis that. the card is re-entering and growing back. into focus. Now, opacity. At the start, the opacity. is set to zero, meaning the card is. fully transparent. As soon as the scroll begins, we fade it. in, increasing opacity up to one. This. happens fairly early in the animation to. make the cards feel like they. materialize softly instead of snapping. in. Then horizontal position and. rotation. Card one is initially pushed. to the right by 100% and rotated by. minus 5°. Card 3 is pushed to the left by minus. 100% and rotated by 5°. Card two stays. centered with no rotation. As you scroll, each card gradually moves. back toward the center. The left and. right cards interpolate their X position. from plus or minus 100% back to 0% and. their rotation value is back to 0°. So. all the cards straighten out and align. neatly side by side. And finally the. flip. This is where the back side of the. card gets revealed. We rotate the card. around its Y-axis. That's the horizontal. axis that creates a card flipping. effect. The rotation goes from 0° to. 180° completing a full half turn. When. it hits 180, the back face is fully. visible. Each card has a flip card in a. wrapper specifically for this flip. So. we can rotate just that part without. affecting the positioning or scale. By. combining these transforms, vertical. drop, scale up, fade in, horizontal. shift, rotation flattening, and finally. the Y-axis flip, we recreate that smooth. magical movement where the cards float. back, regroup in the center, and flip. open to show their content. All of these. changes happen smoothly thanks to our. custom smooth step easing function, and.

are timed across a four screen scroll. range. This gives the animation plenty. of space to breathe and lets each face. happen gradually and clearly. Hope you. found the video helpful. See you in the. next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8MUZcfnuYvA

Learn how to create animated cards using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and ScrollTrigger — complete with scroll-driven slide outs, floating re-entry, and 3D flip effects.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Eduard Bodak

Website: https://www.eduardbodak.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.content:encoded:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 本摘要介紹了一個複雜且視覺效果出色的網頁卡片動畫的實現過程。
  • 它闡述了如何利用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 這兩個強大的 JavaScript 函式庫來創建基於滾動的動畫。
  • 摘要詳細說明了實現此效果所需的 HTML 結構設定（包括克服佈局挑戰的技巧）及 CSS 樣式應用。

摘要

影片的作者在瀏覽網頁設計獎項尋找靈感時，發現了一個作品集網站上令人印象深刻的動畫效果。這個效果展示了卡片如何隨著滾動而滑出、在頁面後續部分重新平滑地浮入、堆疊、然後散開並翻轉以顯示背面內容。作者對這個概念深感著迷。

技術與準備

作者決定親自嘗試重現這個效果，經過約 6 小時的實驗，他成功地使用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 實現了一個非常相似的概念。這個影片的目的，便是要逐步展示如何利用 HTML、CSS、JavaScript、GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 來建構這個精確的滾動動畫。

HTML結構 - 基本設定

作者首先開始建立基本的 HTML 結構，包括一個簡單的導覽列（nav）。接著建立主要視覺區 (hero section)，用於放置動畫開始時會滑出的第一組卡片。這些卡片此時只包含正面。隨後加入一個填充區段 (filler section) 作為簡單的間隔。

HTML結構 - 卡片重新進入技巧

實現卡片重新進入和動畫的部分是關鍵。作者解釋說，如果直接將卡片放在預定的區段，會遇到滾動觸發和固定定位帶來的佈局問題。為了解決這個問題並重現原始動畫的時間點，作者採用了一個巧妙的方案：將一個邏輯上的區段拆分為兩個實體區段。第一個是服務區段 (services section)，只包含標題；第二個是一個獨立的卡片區段 (card section)，它將在整個過程中固定在視口中，並放置在內容圖層的後方。服務區段將設定為透明，以便在滾動時能看到下方的卡片區段。之後會使用 ScrollTrigger 在正確的時間點取消固定卡片區段，創造出一個無縫的整體區段的錯覺。

HTML結構 - 完整卡片與包裹元素

接著作者建構了實際的卡片結構，這些卡片位於固定的卡片區段內。每張卡片都有一個包裹容器 (card wrapper)，內部再包含一個用於翻轉的包裹 (flip card inner)。作者強調這些多層的包裹元素非常重要，它們允許獨立應用不同的變換效果，例如浮動和翻轉，避免動畫之間的干擾。每個內部卡片又分為正面 (flip card front) 和背面 (flip card back)。正面的內容與主要視覺區的卡片保持一致以確保視覺匹配，背面則加入其他內容。最後，頁面底部加入一個結尾區段 (outro section) 完成整個 HTML 結構。

CSS樣式 - 起始設定

進入 CSS 樣式部分。作者先導入了所需的字體，並定義了一些 CSS 變數用於顏色管理。隨後進行了預設樣式重設，移除 margin 和 padding，並設定 box-sizing。

CSS樣式 - 基礎與導覽列

為 body 元素設定了基礎字體樣式，確保整體排版簡潔。接著設定了標題 (H1) 和段落的樣式，並為 span 元素應用特定的標籤樣式（大寫、特定字體、小尺寸）。導覽列 (nav) 被設定為固定在視口頂部並使用 flexbox 進行內部元素的排列。

CSS樣式 - 區段佈局

作者為所有區段定義了基礎樣式，使其佔滿螢幕寬高，並設定內邊距和隱藏溢出。主要視覺區 (hero section) 設定了淺色背景和深色文字，而 about 和 outro 區段則使用深色背景和淺色文字，並使用 flexbox 將內容水平和垂直置中。

CSS樣式 - 主要視覺區卡片

樣式化主要視覺區的卡片容器 (hero cards container)，使用絕對定位並通過 transform 將其置中。容器內部使用 flexbox 水平排列卡片並加入間距。每個卡片設定固定的長寬比、內邊距和圓角，並設定為垂直方向的 flex 容器。卡片內的標題也使用 flexbox 排列。個別的卡片則根據設計需求賦予獨特的背景顏色，並調整 Z-index 和設定變換原點，以便後續動畫控制。

CSS樣式 - 服務與固定卡片區段

為服務區段 (services section) 添加額外的內邊距，並將其標題使用 transform 推到下方，為滾動動畫預留空間。接著樣式化固定在螢幕中的卡片區段 (card section)，將其延伸至全寬高，並通過負 Z-index 使其位於其他元素下方。背景顏色設定為淺色，以匹配主要視覺區。

CSS樣式 - 完整卡片細節

樣式化卡片區段內的卡片容器 (cards container)，使用 flexbox 將其內部內容置中並加入卡片間距和頂部外邊距。該區段內的每個卡片同樣設定長寬比，並添加 3D 透視效果，以便後續應用 3D 翻轉動畫。

CSS樣式 - 包裹元素與翻轉準備

樣式化卡片內部最重要的包裹元素：card wrapper 用於使用絕對定位和 translate 技巧置中內部內容，同時這個包裹層將應用一個簡單的 keyframe 浮動動畫，使其輕微上下移動。flip card inner 包裹層則啟用 preserve-3d 屬性，確保卡片正反面能在 3D 空間中正確翻轉。

CSS樣式 - 卡片正反面

樣式化 flip card front 和 flip card back。兩者都被設定為絕對定位、佔滿父容器空間，並隱藏背面可見性 (backface-visibility: hidden)。它們都有圓角並隱藏溢出。正面設定內邊距並使用 flexbox 垂直排列內容，背景顏色與主要視覺區對應的卡片顏色一致。背面則被初始旋轉 180 度，以便在翻轉時能正確顯示。

================================================

While browsing awards for inspiration, I. came across a portfolio site that had. just one site of the day. Right away, I. was impressed by how polished everything. felt. From the design direction to the. animations and interactions. Out of all. the interactions, this one stood out. The hero cards slide outward as you. scroll, then reappear in a later. section, but not in a typical way. As. you keep scrolling, the cards float. smoothly back into view and settle into. a neat stack. Then with more scroll they. spread across the screen, flip around. and reveal their backsides all while. maintaining the smooth floating motion. throughout. I found the concept. fascinating. So I gave it a shot myself. After about 6 hours of experimenting, I. managed to recreate a very similar. concept using Gap and scroll trigger. You can see everything is there from the. card slide out at the start to the. floating return to the flipping. animation that reveals the content. In. today's video, I'll show you how you can. build this exact scroll animation using. HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and scroll. trigger. If you like seeing such. award-winning web animations broken down. and rebuilt from scratch, give this. video a like and subscribe to the. channel if you haven't already. And if. you want to access the source code for. this project, plus hundreds of other. similar micro projects and a brand new. full website template every month, you. can check out the pro membership via the. link in the description. All right,. let's get into it. Let's start by. setting up the HTML structure. Beginning. with a simple nav bar. I'll add just two. elements inside it for now. One for the. logo and one for the menu button each. wrapped in a span with placeholder text. This part is mostly for visual balance. So feel free to skip it if your focus is. purely on the animation. Next, let's. create the hero section. Inside it, I'll. add a container called hero cards. This. is where we'll place the first set of. cards. Now the idea here is to use these. cards just for the initial slide out. animation. So we'll only add the front. face of each card. Later in the page,. we'll create the full versions of these. cards with both front and back sides. which will slide in from the top to give. the illusion that the same cards are. re-entering in a new section. In. reality, both versions will be separate. elements, but by making their front. faces look identical, we can pull off. that seamless visual trick. So for now. I'll create a div with the class card. and assign it a unique ID. Inside that. I'll add two wrappers with the class. card title and use pen text to show the. card label and index. I'm wrapping them. in divs so we can easily lay them out. using flexbox later. Next I'll duplicate. this card two more times. Change their. ids and update the content accordingly. Giving us three cards in total for the.

hero section. With that done, the hero. cards are ready. Now let's add a simple filler section. below which I'll call about. This will. just have an H1 with some placeholder. text on a black background. Nothing. functional, just for spacing. Then comes. the key part, the section where we want. the cards to re-enter and animate. I'll. call this section services. Here I'll. add a section header div and drop an H1. inside with some placeholder text. Now. technically this is where our full cards. should appear. But if you look at the. original animation, the cards actually. begin sliding in when this section is. already halfway in view. To recreate the. timing, I first tried placing the cards. directly inside this section, but it. didn't work out because I needed to. animate based on scroll progress and. pinning midscroll caused layout issues. If I set the scroll trigger start point. towardly, the pinning happens midway,. which isn't what we want. And if I set. it to start right at the top, then we. lose the entry timing. So the solution I. landed on is to split it into two. sections. The first is the services. section which only holds the header. Then I'll create a separate card section. which will remain fixed in the viewport. the entire time behind the content. layers. We'll make the services section. transparent so the card section appears. visible underneath as the user scrolls. Later using scroll trigger we'll unpin. the card section at the right moment. Just after the services section finishes. spinning to create the illusion that. it's all in one seamless section. Now. let's build the actual cards inside the. card section I'll start by adding a. container called cards container to hold. everything. Then just like before I'll. add a card div with a unique id inside. that I'll create a card wrapper div. which will act as a holder for the. internal content. Then within that I'll. add a div with the class flip cardainer. All of these wrappers are important. because they let us separate different. transformations for floating and. flipping. So, one animation doesn't. interfere with another. You'll see in. the JavaScript later that we use each. rapper to apply specific transforms,. letting us animate position, scale, and. rotation independently. Now, let's split. the inner card into two parts. Flip card. front and flip card back. On the front. side, I'll add the same content we used. in the hero cards, so the two versions. visually match. For the back side, I add. some placeholder content as well. And in. the middle, insert a card copy div. Inside that, I'll list out the service. items using paragraph elements. That's. essentially the full HTML structure for. one card. I'll go ahead and duplicate it. twice, change the IDs and the content,. and we'll have all three cards ready. Finally, I'll add an outro section at. the very end with a simple H1 and some.

placeholder text to close things out. And that's the full HTML structure done. Let's move on to the styling. You can see I've already imported two. fonts from Google Fonts. One is DM Sense. and the other is DM Mono. Next, I have. defined a few CSS variables for colors. This includes a dark shade, a couple of. light neutrals, and three accent colors,. one for each card. Then I'll reset the. default styling using the universal. selector, removing margin and padding,. and setting box sizing to border box. For the body, I'll apply the DMs font to. keep the overall typography clean and. modern. Now, for the heading styles,. I'll keep H1 slightly bold with a medium. large size and give paragraph elements a. comfortable size with a bit of weight as. well. For all the span elements, I'll. make the text uppercase, apply the mono. font, and set a smaller size with medium. weight to give it that label-like look. Moving on to the nav, I'll fix it at the. top of the screen, make it stretch full. width, and add some padding. Then I'll. use flexbox to space out the logo and. the menu button. Both the logo and menu span elements. will get some padding and a slight. border radius. The logo gets a dark background with. light text and the menu gets the. opposite light background with dark. text. Next, I'll define some base styles for. every section. Each section will be set. to take full width and height of the. screen with a bit of padding and. overflow hidden to help with the. animations. For the hero section, I'll. set the background to a light color and. the text to dark for good contrast. The about and out sections will use a. dark background with light text. And. I'll center their content both. vertically and horizontal using flexbox. Now let's style the hero cards. container. I'll absolutely position it. in the center of the screen using top. left and transform and give it a fixed. width. I'll use flexbox to align the. cards horizontally with some spacing in. between. Each card inside hero cards will have a. fixed aspect ratio, padding, and border. radius. I'll also set them to be column flex. containers spaced from top to bottom. The card title inside each card will be. styled as a horizontal flex row with. space between the span elements. The. span themselves will get a smaller font. size to give that minimalist design. feel. For the individual hero cards,. I'll assign them their unique accent. background colors. I'll also adjust. their Zindex and set transform origin. for the left and right cards to control. how they animate later. Now for each services section, I'll give. it extra padding to leave enough room. for scroll interaction. Inside it, the. services header will be positioned.

relative, center aligned, and pushed way. down using a transform. This will allow. us to animate it into view using scroll. later. I also will change so the browser. can optimize that animation. Now we'll style the card section. This. one is fixed to the screen and stretched. full width and height sitting behind. everything else using a negative Z. index. The background is set to light to match. the hero section. Inside it, the cards container will be. centered using flexbox with some spacing. between the cards and a bit of top. margin so they don't sit too high. Each card in this section will again. follow the same aspect ratio. I'm also. adding a 3D perspective here so we can. apply the flip animations in 3D space. Now for the card wrapper, this is where. I'll center the inner content using. absolute positioning and a translate. trick. I'll also apply floating animation to. this wrapper. It's a simple key frame animation that. gently moves the card up and down on a. loop to offset the motion slightly for. each card. I'll add a delay to the. animation for second and third cards. Next, the flip card inner will have. preserve 3D enabled so that both front. and back faces can flipped in 3D without. flattening. Flip card front and flip card back will. be absolutely positioned, take full size. and have hidden back faces. I'm also giving them a border radius and. hiding overflow. The front side will have some padding. and use flexbox to space the titles. vertically. The background colors here will match. the same accent colors as their hero. counterparts. For the back face, I'll rotate it by. 180° so it flips into view when we. trigger the rotation later. It gets the. same padding and layout, but the. background is set to white. I've also. added a bit of spacing between its. elements using a gap. Inside the back face, the card copy. container will be a vertical stack with. a small gap between each item. Each paragraph inside will be centered. with a readable font size, a light. background, and slightly rounded. corners. Now I'll define the initial transforms. for each of the three cards. Card one is. pushed off to the right and slightly. rotated. Guard two sits in the middle. with no rotation. Guard three is pushed. off to the left with a slight positive. rotation. I'm also scaling all three. down so they animate up into full size. later. Finally, I'll set the default. opacity of all cards to zero so they. fade in as we scroll. And that wraps up. the CSS part. Next, we'll move into the. animation part using JavaScript and Gab. Let's start by setting up the basics.

First, I'm importing Gap along with the. scroll trigger plugin and Lennis, which. we'll be using to add smooth scrolling. Once the page finishes loading, I'll. register scroll trigger so we can start. using it in our animations. Then I'll initialize Lennis and connect. it to scroll trigger. This makes sure. that every time we scroll with Lennis,. scroll trigger is aware of the scroll. position and updates accordingly. To. keep everything in sync, I'll also hook. Lennis into Gap's internal animation. loop. This ensures the scroll position. gets updated on every frame, giving a. super smooth performance. Next, I define. a small helper function. This function. smooths out the progress values we get. from scroll trigger, easing them in and. out to make our animations feel more. natural. We'll use this smoothing. technique throughout the rest of the. code. Now, let's animate the hero cards. as user scrolls. I'm creating a scroll. trigger that watches the hero section. The animation starts when the top of the. hero section hits the top of the screen. and ends when about 3/4 of that section. has scrolled past. I have enabled. scrubbing here which means the animation. will play in sync with the scroll. progress. The slower you scroll, the. slower the animation runs and vice. versa. Inside the onupdate call back, I. first grab the progress value. This will. be a number between 0 and one. representing how far we have scrolled. through the animation range. Then I use. that progress to slowly fade out the. entire hero cards container. The opacity. starts at full and fades to about half. by the time the section scrolls out. Next, I animate each of the three cards. individually. I loop through them one by. one and give each card a slight delay so. that animations feel staggered. To. calculate how much each card should. move, I take the scroll progress and. offset it slightly based on the card's. index. This gives us a unique progress. value for each card so they don't. animate at the exact same time. Then I. animate their vertical movement. As you. scroll, each card moves downwards. starting from its original position and. floating all the way down off screen. At. the same time, I scale each card down. starting at its original size and. gradually shrinking it to about 3/4. Now, for the horizontal movement and. rotation, this is where we separate the. cards visually. The middle card stays. centered with no rotation, but the first. card moves to the right and rotates. slightly counterclockwise, while the. third card moves to the left and rotates. clockwise. The subtle offsets give that. fanned out sliding effect that makes the. whole interaction feel dynamic and. layered. All of these animations are. tied to scroll smoothed out using that. easing function we defined earlier which. makes the transitions feel soft and. fluid. So as the user scrolls past the.

hero section, the cards gently fade out,. float down, shift apart, and scale down. setting the stage for them to reappear. later. Now, let's set up the next part. of the scroll animation where the. services section takes over and cards. begin to re-enter. First, I'm creating a. scroll trigger on the services section. This trigger starts when the top of the. section hits the top of the screen and. runs for about four screen heights. So,. we have enough vertical space to animate. everything smoothly. I'm also pinning. the services section in place during. that range. This means as you scroll,. the section will stay fixed while the. rest of the animation plays out on top. of it. That's the moment where the cards. begin to float back in. Now, here's the. important part. You might remember how. our actual animated cards are placed in. a completely separate section called. cards. We want those cards to appear. behind the services section and move in. perfect sync with it so the user never. realizes they are from a different. section. To make that illusion work, I'm. creating another scroll trigger on the. same range. As soon as the services. section scrolls out of view, meaning. it's no longer pinned, I switch the. position of card section from fixed to. absolute, this anchors the cards in. place at the exact point where the. services section left off. I do this by. grabbing the top position of the. services section and setting the card. section to that same spot on the page. This allows the cards to scroll along. naturally with the rest of the content. as we move forward. Now when we scroll back up, I reverse. that. I set the card section back to. fixed so it's ready to play the. animation again in reverse. This back. and forth positioning lets us animate. the cards freely while keeping them. visually locked to the services section. even though technically they are in. different layer behind it. Now we are. setting up the most important part. The. scroll animation that brings the card. back into view, spreads them out and. flips them to reveal their content. We. are creating a scroll trigger on the. services section. The animation starts. when the section just enters the bottom. of the screen and it runs for a distance. equal to four full screen heights. Enough room to stretch out and control. each movement gradually. Scrubbing is. enabled here which means the animation. is directly linked to scroll. Plays. forward as you scroll down and reverses. as you scroll back up. Every pixel of. scroll controls the timing. Inside the. on-update call back, we grab the scroll. progress, a value that goes from 0 to. one. Depending on how far we have. scrolled through that triggers range. The first thing we animate is the header. inside the services section. It starts. way below its original position, offset. by 400%, which means it's pushed down by.

four times its height. As you scroll, we. ease that value back down to 0%,. bringing the header into view from. below. It moves smoothly because we are. applying that easing function we defined. earlier. Now we move on to animating the. three cards. We loop through each card. and introduce a slight delay for each. one using its index. This delay is. created by subtracting a small value. from the scroll progress based on the. index. Let's go through the transformations we. are applying to each card. First. vertical position. Each card starts. fully off screen above the viewport. offset by minus 100%. As the scroll. progresses, the car drops down to the. middle at 50%. Then finally settles into place at 0%. This gives that floating decent effect. as if the cards are drifting back down. into view. Next, scaling. We start with each card. scaled down to 25 or 25% of its full. size. As the scroll progresses, it grows to 75. and eventually scales up to one, which. is its original size. This gradual scaling helps emphasis that. the card is re-entering and growing back. into focus. Now, opacity. At the start, the opacity. is set to zero, meaning the card is. fully transparent. As soon as the scroll begins, we fade it. in, increasing opacity up to one. This. happens fairly early in the animation to. make the cards feel like they. materialize softly instead of snapping. in. Then horizontal position and. rotation. Card one is initially pushed. to the right by 100% and rotated by. minus 5°. Card 3 is pushed to the left by minus. 100% and rotated by 5°. Card two stays. centered with no rotation. As you scroll, each card gradually moves. back toward the center. The left and. right cards interpolate their X position. from plus or minus 100% back to 0% and. their rotation value is back to 0°. So. all the cards straighten out and align. neatly side by side. And finally the. flip. This is where the back side of the. card gets revealed. We rotate the card. around its Y-axis. That's the horizontal. axis that creates a card flipping. effect. The rotation goes from 0° to. 180° completing a full half turn. When. it hits 180, the back face is fully. visible. Each card has a flip card in a. wrapper specifically for this flip. So. we can rotate just that part without. affecting the positioning or scale. By. combining these transforms, vertical. drop, scale up, fade in, horizontal. shift, rotation flattening, and finally. the Y-axis flip, we recreate that smooth. magical movement where the cards float. back, regroup in the center, and flip. open to show their content. All of these. changes happen smoothly thanks to our. custom smooth step easing function, and.

are timed across a four screen scroll. range. This gives the animation plenty. of space to breathe and lets each face. happen gradually and clearly. Hope you. found the video helpful. See you in the. next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8MUZcfnuYvA

Learn how to create animated cards using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and ScrollTrigger — complete with scroll-driven slide outs, floating re-entry, and 3D flip effects.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Eduard Bodak

Website: https://www.eduardbodak.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!

item.title: This 3D Scroll Animation Makes Your Product Page Feel Alive (GSAP & Three.js)
item.itunes:title: This 3D Scroll Animation Makes Your Product Page Feel Alive (GSAP & Three.js)
item.itunes:author: Codegrid
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 學習如何使用 Three.js 將 3D 模型整合到網頁中。
  • 掌握 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 庫，創建與捲動連動的複雜動畫。
  • 了解如何重現曾獲獎項的精美網頁動畫效果，包括跑馬燈文字、圓形遮罩和提示文字動畫。
  • 學習如何使用 SplitText 插件來精細控制文字的進場動畫。

摘要

這段影片中，作者分享了他在尋找靈感時，發現一個獲得當日最佳網站獎項的精美網站。網站中一段流暢、執行精良的動畫序列立刻吸引了他的目光，這段動畫就像是一個動態的產品時間軸，頁面中央有一個 3D 模型隨著使用者的捲動平滑旋轉，周圍則有醒目的文字滑入，接著是一個平滑過渡到下一個場景的圓形遮罩，然後是另一個跑馬燈效果，最後是一組與捲動精確綁定的提示文字，所有這些元素都在一個無縫的部分中完成。這種乾淨、周到且視覺引人入勝的組合方式，讓作者決定要將其重現。

為此，作者在 Sketchfab 上找到一個免費且非常符合概念的 3D 模型，並下載了 GLTF 格式。他使用這個模型，並搭配 GSAP、ScrollTrigger 和 Three.js，重建了類似的體驗，包含了所有關鍵動畫元素。在影片中，作者將逐步引導觀眾學習如何將任何 3D 模型整合到網頁中，並利用 GSAP 和 ScrollTrigger 製作沈浸式的捲動體驗。

HTML 結構設定

作者從基本的 HTML 結構開始建置。他創建了三個主要的區塊：介紹區 (intro)、產品概覽區 (product overview) 和結尾區 (outro)，每個都有唯一的 class。介紹區和結尾區只放入簡單的標題，讓頁面不至於太空。真正的重點在於產品概覽區，所有動畫都將在這裡呈現。首先，他加入了一個 class 為 header one 的 div 和一個 h1 標題，這是第一個跑馬燈風格的文字。緊接著是 class 為 header two 的 div，作為動畫時間軸中的後續文字。之後，他添加了一個空的 div 作為圓形遮罩 (circular mask)，用於在第一個標題捲出後，產生放大的深色圓形背景效果。接下來是建立提示文字區 (tool tip section)，他將所有提示文字包裹在一個 class 為 tool tips 的 div 中，每個提示文字包含四個部分：圖標、分隔線、標題和簡短描述。最後，他加入一個空的 div 作為 3D 模型的容器 (model container)。至此，基本的 HTML 結構設定完成。

CSS 樣式設計

接著，作者開始使用 CSS 進行樣式設計。他導入了新的 Montreal 字體並應用基本的重設樣式，移除預設的邊距和內邊距，並設定所有元素的 box-sizing 為 border-box。然後將字體應用到整個 body。他設定了 h1、h2 和段落的字體大小、粗細和行高，以確保排版一致且易讀。每個區塊都被設定為佔滿螢幕寬度和高度，有淺色背景、深色文字並隱藏溢出內容。介紹區和結尾區使用了 flexbox 置中內容，並設定黑色背景和白色文字，使其突出並框住主要內容。模型容器被絕對定位在螢幕中心，佔滿寬高並設定較高的 z-index 以確保它在其他元素之上。header one 被設定為相對定位，寬度為螢幕的兩倍，準備用於水平動畫。header two 被固定在左上角，初始位置偏右，用於後續進場動畫。圓形遮罩初始是完全隱藏的，利用 clip-path 實現。提示文字容器被 flexbox 置中，大小約為區塊的四分之三，包含兩個水平間隔開的提示文字。每個提示文字中的元素垂直排列，並設定間距。分隔線是細水平線，初始隱藏，將用 GSAP 動畫呈現。文字部分增加了間距並限制了第二個提示文字元素的寬度，以創建不對稱佈局。為了準備動畫，他為標題和描述分配了特定的 class 名稱，以便後續使用 GSAP 的 SplitText 插件定位。最後，他添加了響應式樣式，在小螢幕上調整字體大小、圖標大小，並將提示文字垂直堆疊並重新置中，確保佈局在不同裝置上保持整潔。

JavaScript 與動畫準備

進入 JavaScript 部分，作者首先導入了專案所需的所有庫：Three.js (用於 3D 渲染)、GLTFLoader (載入 GLTF 格式的 3D 模型)、GSAP (動畫庫)、ScrollTrigger (與捲動連動的動畫插件)、SplitText (分割文字的插件) 以及 Lennis (平滑捲動庫)。在 DOM 準備就緒後，他註冊了 GSAP 的 ScrollTrigger 和 SplitText 插件，並初始化 Lennis。Lennis 監聽原生捲動事件，提供更平滑的捲動體驗，並透過 GSAP 的 ticker 更新，確保捲動與動畫同步。他關閉了 GSAP 內部的平滑處理，特別是針對長捲動動畫。接著，作者使用 SplitText 插件處理文字，為第一個跑馬燈文字分割成單個字符，並賦予自訂 class 以便後續定位動畫。對於提示文字的標題和描述，則按行分割。每個字符和行都被包裹在 span 標籤中，以便對每個文字片段進行精細控制，包括交錯過渡和自訂運動效果。隨後，他定義了一個名為 animOptions 的配置物件，包含了預設的動畫持續時間、easing 曲線和小的交錯值，避免重複寫入相同的設置。他還定義了一個名為 tooltipSelectors 的陣列，用於儲存每個提示文字動畫組的配置，包括觸發動畫的捲動進度百分比。最後，作者為第一個跑馬燈標題創建了 ScrollTrigger，設定當產品概覽區塊的頂部進入視口 75% 時觸發動畫，將標題中每個 span 包裹的字符從下方滑入視圖，創造流暢的字母進場效果。

================================================

[Music]. While hunting for inspiration for a new. video, I stumbled upon this stunning. website that won awards site of the day. back in January. As I scrolled through. it, one section instantly caught my eye. This clean, well-executed animation. sequence that felt like a dynamic. timeline for the product. Right in the. center of the page, a 3D model rotates. smoothly as the user scrolls. Around it,. bold mary text slides in, followed by a. circular mask that smoothly transitions. to the next scene. then another marquee. and finally a set of tool tip texts that. animate in each one tied precisely to. your scroll all within a single seamless. section. The way it was all put. together, clean, thoughtful, and. visually engaging made it clear this was. something I wanted to rebuild. So I. hopped on Sketch Fab and found a free 3D. model that fit the concept perfectly. I'll drop the link in the description if. you want to grab it. You can download it. in the GLP format. Using that model, I. built out a similar experience using. GAP, scroll trigger, and 3JS, bringing. in all the key animations you saw. earlier. In today's video, I'll walk you. through how to take any 3D model and. integrate it into your web page using. 3JS, then use Gab and Scroll Trigger to. animate everything around it for a truly. immersive scroll experience. If you. enjoy award-winning web animations. broken down and rebuilt from scratch,. give this video a like and subscribe if. you haven't already. And if you want to. access the source code for this project,. plus hundreds of other similar micro. projects and a brand new full website. template every month, you can check out. the pro membership via the link in the. description. All right, let's get into. it. Let's start by setting up the basic. HTML structure. I'll create three main. sections, an intro, a product overview,. and an outro, each with its own unique. class. Inside the intro and outro. sections, I'll just drop in a simple. heading using H1 tag. Nothing fancy,. just enough content so the page doesn't. feel empty. Now the real focus is the. product overview section. This is where. all of our animations will live. First,. I'll add a div with the class header one. and place an H1 inside it. This will be. the first marquee style text that. appears when the section gets pinned. Right after that, I'll add another div. called header 2 with a second heading. This will slide in as the follow-up text. in the animation timeline. Next, I'm. adding an empty div with the class. circular mask. This will serve as the. dark circular background that scales up. after the first header scrolls out. Now. it's time to build the tool tip section. I'll wrap everything inside a div called. tool tips. Each tool tip will have four.

parts. An icon, a divider, a title, and. a short description. For the icon, I'll. use iron icon, but you can use any other. icon or image you want. The divider will. just be an empty div for now. We'll. style it later with CSS. Then I'll add a. title using H2 and a short paragraph for. the description text. Once that's set. up, I'll duplicate the entire tool tip. block to add a second one and swap out. the content. Finally, we need a container to render. the 3D model. So, I'll add an empty div. with the class model container. And that. wraps up the HTML structure. Let's move. on to styling it with CSS. First, I'll import the new Montreal font. and apply a basic reset. I'll remove all. default margins and paddings and set box. sizing to border box for every element. Then I'll apply the font to the entire. body so the typography stays consistent. across the layout. Next, I'll style the headings and. paragraphs. For H1, I'll go with a large. size, medium weight, and tight line. spacing. For H2, I'll slightly reduce. the size, add tighter letter spacing,. and keep the line height balanced. And for the paragraph, I'll keep it. simple, medium weight, and a relaxed. line height for better readability. Now I'll set up the base section layout. Each section will take up the full width. and height of the screen. I'll give it a. light background color, dark text, and. hide any overflow so it doesn't. interfere with the scroll-based. animations. For the intro and outro. sections, I'll center everything using. flexbox and give them a black background. with white text. This will help them. stand out and frame the main content. Next, I'll set up the model container. I'll position it absolutely at the. center of the screen using top, left,. and transform. It will stretch across the full width. and height and sit on a high index to. make sure it renders above other. elements. Now, let's take care of the. headers. For header one, I'll make it. relatively positioned and twice the. width of the screen. This is the one. we'll animate horizontally like a. marquee. Then for the header two, I'll fix it to. the top left of the screen and start it. off to the right. This one will animate. in later and set up everything else. using Z-index. Both headers will use flexbox to. vertically center the content. inside them. I'll use huge bold type for. that bold animated text field. Now I'll style the circular mask. This. is a full section element that starts. completely hidden using a circular clip. path set to 0%. I'll animate this later to create that. dramatic circular reveal effect.

Time to move on to the tool tips. I'll. center the entire tool tips container. using flexbox and size it to about 3/4. of the section. Inside it, I'll create. two tool tips spaced far apart. horizontally. Each tool tip will have. four elements, an icon, a divider, a. title, and a description. I'll stack. them vertically with some spacing. The. first tool tip will align to the top. left by default. For the second one, I'll push it down. and to the right using justify content. and align items. For the icon, I'll scale it up for. visibility. The divider will be a thin horizontal. line. I'll keep it hidden by default. with a 0x scale and animated later with. gap. I'll also set the transform origin. so one expands from the right and the. other from the left. For the text, I'll add consistent. spacing and limit the width of the. second tool tips elements to create an. offset asymmetrical layout. Now, to prep for animations, I'll assign. specific class names to the headers,. titles, and descriptions so we can. target them later using Gap's split text. plug-in. We'll use JavaScript to break. the text into individual lines or. characters during the runtime, not. manually in HTML. Finally, I'll add. responsive styles using a media query. On smaller screens, I'll reduce the font. sizes for headings and icons. I'll stack. the tool tips vertically and recenter. everything to keep the layout clean. I'll also reset alignment on the second. tool tip so it flows naturally in the. vertical layout. Divider widths and. transform origins will adjust as needed. to keep things smooth across devices. That wraps up the CSS. Now the layout is. fully styled and animation ready. So. let's jump into the JavaScript. All right. First, I'm going to import. all the libraries we'll need for this. project. I'll import three to handle the. 3D rendering and gltf loader so we can. load our 3D model in GLP format. Next,. I'll bring in GSA for animations along. with scroll trigger for scroll based. motion and split text for breaking up. text into lines or characters so we can. animate them later. And finally, I'll. import Lennis. This is the smooth scroll. library we'll be using to enhance scroll. behavior across the entire page. Once. the DOM is ready, I'll register the GSAP. plugins. We are going to use scroll. trigger and split text. Then I'll initialize Lennis. Lennis. listens to the native scroll and gives. us a smoother, more fluid scrolling. experience. I'll hook it up to Gap. sticker, which means Lennis will be. updated on every animation frame. I'm. also turning off Gap's internal lag. smoothing so everything stays in sync,.

especially for long scroll-based. animations. Now, let's prepare our text. for animation. I'll use the split text. plug-in on the first header, which is. the marker text. Here, I'll split the. text into individual characters and. assign each one a custom class so we can. target them with animations later. Then,. I'll do the same for the tool tip titles. and descriptions, but instead of. splitting them by character, I'll split. them line by line. This will help us. animate each line in sequence. Finally, I'll wrap each character and. each line in a span. This is important. By wrapping the text manually in spans,. I get full control over the animation. for each piece of text, including. stagger transitions and custom motion. effects that sets up our scroll text and. plug-in configuration. In the next part,. we'll dive into the scroll trigger logic. and the 3D model setup. So, next I'll. define some animation options we'll. reuse throughout this project. I'm. creating a config object called anim. options. It defines the default. animation duration, easing curve, and a. small stagger value. This way I don't. have to rewrite these settings every. time I call gap animation. Next, I'll. define an array called tooltip. selectors. Each object in this array. represents a tool tip animation group. I'll assign it a trigger value. This is. the scroll progress percentage at which. I want each tool tip to animate in. For. example, when the scroll reaches around. 65%, I want to animate the icon, title,. lines, and description lines of the. first tool tip. And then at around 85% I. trigger the second tool tips animation. in the same way. This setup makes the. tool tip animation timeline modular and. clean. I can easily loop over these. groups later and animate each set based. on scroll progress. Now I'll create a. scroll trigger for the first marquee. header, the one inside header one. This. trigger activates when the product. overview section enters the view port. from bottom specifically when it stop at. 75% of the screen. When that happens,. I'll animate all the span wrapped. characters inside the first shredder by. sliding them up into view from below. That gives that smooth letter by letter. rolling effect we saw earlier. I'll also. define what happens if the user scrolls. back up. When the section scrolls out in. reverse, I'll animate the same. characters downward again, resetting. their position and allowing the. animation to replay if needed. That. takes care of the animated entrance for. our first setting. In the next part,. I'll move on to setting up the 3D scene. using 3JS. First, I'll declare a few. variables to store our model reference,. its size, and a rotation tracker. I'll. use this later to position and animate.

the model accurately during scroll. Next, I'll create a new scene. This acts. as a container that holds everything we. render, lights, models, cameras, all of. it. Then, I'll create a perspective. camera. I'm using a field of view of. 60°. And I'm calculating the aspect. ratio based on the current window size. I'm also defining the near and far. clipping planes so 3JS knows what range. of depth to render. After that, I'll set. up the WebGL renderer. I'll enable. anti-aliasing for smoother edges and. make the background transparent by. setting alpha to true. Then I'll. configure the renderer. I'll clear out. the background to transparent black. I'll set its size to match the full. window and make sure it respects high. resolution screens by clamping the pixel. ratio to a max of two. I'll enable shadows and specify the type. of shadow map for better quality. I'll also apply basic tone mapping and. output encoding to make sure lighting. looks natural. Finally, I'll append the render to the. model container div in our HTML so it. shows up in the right section. Now it's time to light the scene. I'll. add a soft ambient light to brighten up. the entire model uniformly. Then I'll add a directional light that. acts as the sunlight. I'll position it at an angle, enable. shadows, and fine-tune the shadow bias. and map size for performance and visual. quality. to fill in the darker areas and reduce. harsh shadows. I'll also add a second. directional light on the opposite side. with a lower intensity. This helps create a more balanced. professional lighting setup. Now let's. handle model positioning. I'll write a. function called setup model that. positions the 3D model based on its size. and the screen dimensions. Inside this. function, I'll first check if the model. and its size are available. Then I'll. use 3JS box utility to calculate the. bounding box of the model and I'll. extract the center point. If the screen is mobile sized, I'll. shift the model slightly to the right. Otherwise, I'll push it to the left so. it aligns nicely with the tool tip. layout. I'll also adjust the model's. vertical position by nudging it up. slightly. This makes sure it feels. visually centered in the view port. You. might need to play around this value. when you use another model. For. rotation, I'll tilt the model slightly. on desktop using a negative Z rotation. and leave it straight on mobile. Then. I'll position the camera based on the. model size. I'll calculate the distance. using the largest dimensions of the. model's bonding box and multiply it by a. factor closer on desktop and slightly.

farther on mobile to keep the model. nicely framed. Finally, I'll position. the camera toward the center of the. scene so the model stays in focus. Now. let's load the actual 3D model using. gltf loader. I'll load the file called. shaker.gb. Once the model loads, I'll store the. scene inside our model variable. I'll. loop through each node inside the model. and update its material settings,. reducing metalness and increasing. roughness to give it a more matte. plastic-like appearance, which works. better for this product style. Then I'll. use box 3 again to measure the model's. dimensions and store it size. This is. important for responsive positioning. Once that's done, I'll add the model to. the scene and call our setup model. function to position it correctly. Now. I'll define our animation loop. I'll use. request animation frame to call the. animate function continuously. Inside. it, I'll render the scene using the. current camera settings. This will keep. our 3D view updating smoothly. Lastly,. I'll add a resize event listener. If the. window size changes, I'll update the. camera suspect ratio and projection. metrics. Resize the render and rerun. setup model function so the model always. stays properly framed no matter what. device or orientation the user is on. That completes our 3JS setup. In the. next part, we'll connect everything to. the scroll timeline and start animating. the model and UI based on scroll. progress. I'll create a scroll trigger. instance that targets the product. overview section. I want this section to. pin in place and drive all animations. based on scroll progress. I'll start the. trigger when the top of the section hits. the top of the viewport. And I'll set. the end point to extend 10 full viewport. heights below. This gives us a long. scroll distance to work with for smooth. drawn out transitions. I'll enable. pinning so the section stays fixed. during the scroll period. And I'll keep. pin spacing enabled to maintain layout. flow. Next, I'll turn on scrub, which. links scroll progress directly to the. animation timing. That way everything. moves in sync with how fast or slow the. user scrolls. Inside the on-update call. back, I'll receive a progress value. This value ranges from 0 to one and. tells me exactly how far we are through. the scroll timeline. Now I start. animating based on the scroll progress. First up is the horizontal scroll for. header one. I want this text to scroll. from right to left as the page. progresses. To control the range, I'll. calculate header progress by normalizing. the scroll range from 5% to 35%. I'll. clamp the result between 0 and one using. max and main methods. Then I'll shift. the header using X% starting at zero and.

moving to 100%. This makes the text more. completely off screen to the left as. user scroll progresses. Next, I'll. animate the circular mask that scales up. between 20% and 30% of the scroll. I'll. calculate the size of the mask based on. where we are in that specific range. Below 20%, the mask stays hidden. Above. 30%, it's fully expanded. In between. I'll scale it proportionally using a. linear interpolation. Then I'll apply. the mask size using the clip path. property with a circular value centered. on the screen.
